<a id="t-a2c6999b95"></a>
## Thread: Can cobol be framed-up legally?... — 24 msgs

### Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T13:04:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello Everyone,

when creating a graphical window program -- my window procedure
receives windows's messages such as the following: WM-CREAT,
WM-PAINT, WM-ERASEBKGND, WM-CONTEXTMENU, WM-COMMAND and
WM-DESTROY. So, if I want to handle the message my self, I need
to call an entry-point function and pass parameters such as
lnk-hWnd, lnk-hDC and lnk-hPs which are numeric values. However,
I don't have a Frame-Of-Reference conditions that helps me
determines how to pass these parameters to the entry function.
should I pass these parameters as 'by value' or 'by reference'?
I am using Net Express 3.1 with windows 2000 professional.

your kind help is appreciated. Kellie.

*>--- case in point cobol code -----

linkage section.
01 lnk-hWnd        hWnd.
01 lnk-hDC         hDC.
01 lnk-hPs         hDC.
01 lnk-hDCbitmap   HANDLE.

procedure division.
0001-main.

   ...............

entry "MyWndProc" WinAPI using
                  by value lnk-hwnd
                  by value lnk-iMessage
                  by value lnk-wParam
                  by value lnk-lParam.
     move 0 to ls-mResult.
     evaluate lnk-iMessage

     when WM-PAINT
          call winapi "PaintMyWindow" using
                 by reference lnk-hwnd
                 by reference lnk-hDCbitmap
                 by value     lnk-hps
              returning       return-code
          end-call

      ...............

entry "PaintMyWindow" winapi using
          by reference lnk-hwnd
          by reference lnk-ppaint
          by value     lnk-hps.

      ...............

   exit program returning 0.

end program window.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T22:11:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6UvOd.28911$by5.21742@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
> when creating a graphical window program -- my window procedure
> receives windows's messages such as the following: WM-CREAT,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> should I pass these parameters as 'by value' or 'by reference'?

???

If you are calling your own functions (entrypoints) from your window
procedure, YOU define the number, type, byref/byvalue and calling
convention. You'd select BY VALUE or BY REFERENCE based on your application
requirements.

If you are calling other Windows API functions, those calls are all
documented in the Windows API documentation. If you don't have your own API
reference, you can use the on-line reference starting at
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/default.asp
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T15:32:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1107991973.469364.283940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6UvOd.28911$by5.21742@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <6UvOd.28911$by5.21742@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Hi Michael,

I am Only referring to the following Four lines:

entry "PaintMyWindow" winapi using
          by reference lnk-hWnd
          by reference lnk-pPaint
          by value       lnk-hPs.

the rest of the function code is just some simple win32 API's which is
coded correctly and has no problems. 

thanks for the reply. Kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T00:49:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CcyOd.2269$1m.1272@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <6UvOd.28911$by5.21742@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1107991973.469364.283940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:1107991973.469364.283940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
> Hi Michael,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't understand the question. Your Entry wants three parameters,  2 by
reference, one by value.  According to the linkage for those items, they are
all 32-bit integers (hwnd, DC, and wParam are always 32-bit integers on
32-bit Windows). So that's the way you call it...

CALL PaintMyWindow using
    by reference (hWnd, which is 32-bit integer)
    by reference   (hDC
    by value       hps

Except "hPS" is not a good name, because it implies a handle. in context,
lparam is a POINTER to a "paintstruct" structure. (No such thing as a handle
to a paintstruct, because paintstruct is not an object like a window or
device context).

I assume you have a group item for a paintstruct somewhere.. this is it in
BASIC...
TYPE PAINTSTRUCT
  hDC AS DWORD
  fErase AS LONG
  rcPaint AS RECT
  fRestore AS LONG
  fIncUpdate AS LONG
  rgbReserved(0 TO 31) AS BYTE
END TYPE

So I guess in COBOL it would be
01 ws-paintstruct.
   05  hdc      PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
    05 ferase   PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
    05 rcpaint.
           10  Left     PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
           10  Top     PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
           10  Right     PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
           10  Bottom   PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
    05  Restore    PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
    05  fIncUpdate  PIC S9(9) USAGE BINARY
    05  rgb   PIC X(01) OCCURS 32

01. Stuff
     05 Paintstruct-ptr   USAGE POINTER

when you get the painstruct pointer in lparam...

SET PaintStruct-ptr   TO lparam
SET ADDRESS OF ws-PaintStruct to Paintstruct-ptr

Now just call your function with BY REFERENCE WS-PAINTSTRUCT (which is how I
would have defined the entry point function)

(Hmm, I guess you could have just overridden it in the call by using lparam
BY VALUE...but you'd have to change your linkage .. depends on how many
calls you are making where you are passing  a poiter to a paintstruct if
this is worth the effort).

What I really do not understand is why you are calling an entry point
function you have created to process WM_PAINT... you'd normally just...

WHEN WM_PAINT
    CALL BeginPaint  (gives you a dc and a paintstruct,)
    do all your drawing here on hDC  (hDC is returned by BeginPaint) or
PERFORM a paragraph which does it..
    CALL EndPaint  << don't forget this or you'll get a GDI resource leak..
or at least you will until you exhaust the available device contexts at
which point you'll probably get a GPF when you try to draw on an invalid DC)

Maybe I'm missing something here, but seems to me you are going at this the
hard way.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T20:56:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108011418.204942.274070@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<CcyOd.2269$1m.1272@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <6UvOd.28911$by5.21742@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1107991973.469364.283940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <CcyOd.2269$1m.1272@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Hi Michael,

even though the linkage section shows a 32-bit integers -- the call can
be formatted as
by value or by reference, based on windows's message (wm-whatever).
When it has
to be 'by value' format but I use 'by reference' -- I would get a
runtime error # 114,
which states the following message:

"Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory"  Fatal
Memory access violation has been detected by your operating system.

then, if I reverse the format to the opposite, no error message.

thanks for your kind help and efforts.  Regards.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

#### ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** patrick.magee@microfocus.com
- **Date:** 2005-02-11T05:03:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
Kellie, all your parameters appear to be "handles" (32 bit numeric
values) so they should be passed "by value".

COBOL record definitions (structures) can be passed "by reference". An
alternative would be to define a " usage pointer" item (a 32 bit
numeric value) that you would initialize with a "set" statement and
pass "by value".

Character strings (usually null terminated) would also be passed "by
reference" or the pointer alternative described above.

When you use "by reference" the compiler is creating a pointer item for
you , initializing the item and passing the pointer value "by value".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-11T13:40:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EB2Pd.29358$by5.20399@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
<patrick.magee@microfocus.com> wrote in message
news:1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
> Kellie, all your parameters appear to be "handles" (32 bit numeric
> values) so they should be passed "by value".

Kellie is calling her own entry point function, so the 'byvalueness' or
'byreferenceness' is defined strictly by the USING clause of her ENTRY
statement.  "Standard Windows Behavior" only applies to "Standard Windows
API Functions"

Also see my note re "hPs" .. a Paintstruct is not a windows object like a
window or a device context and therefore there is no handle to it. Windows
functions which accept and/or return a paintstruct (e.g., BeginPaint) always
do so by reference.

MCM
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-11T10:48:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108147728.429566.12150@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108139426.269362.167940@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <EB2Pd.29358$by5.20399@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1108139426.269362.167940@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
> When I used the perform statement in the windows's
> procedure section, the cobol executable was not doing what it
> should be doing, it was hanging and acting weird.

PERFORMs are not the problem.  What may be an issue is that ENTRYs are
_not_ paragraph labels.  ENTRYs do not terminate the scope of a
PERFORM.  If you don't put a paragraph label prior to an ENTRY then a
PERFORM of the paragrph above will drop through the ENTRY.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-11T13:03:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108155816.738551.209300@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108152144.570700.173520@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <EB2Pd.29358$by5.20399@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1108139426.269362.167940@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1108147728.429566.12150@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1108152144.570700.173520@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
>can you please give me an example on how to
>put a Paragraph Label prior to an entry-point as
>you have mentioned above.  Thanks

procedure division.
0001-main.

   ...............

entry "MyWndProc" WinAPI using
                  by value lnk-hwnd
                  by value lnk-iMessage
                  by value lnk-wParam
                  by value lnk-lParam.
     move 0 to ls-mResult.
     evaluate lnk-iMessage

     when WM-PAINT
             PERFORM PaintMyWindow

     when somethingelse

     end-evaluate
     ....
     exit program.

PaintMyWindow.
    .....

SomeOtherLabel.
entry "someOtherEntry" winapi using
          by reference lnk-hwnd
          by reference lnk-ppaint
          by value     lnk-hps.

      ...............

   exit program returning 0.

end program window.

If 'SomeOtherLabel' was not coded the the PERFORM PaintMyWindows would
not end when it readed the ENTRY "someOtherEntry" and would continue to
execute the code there, probably reaching the EXIT PROGRAM in that
rather than returning to the WM-PAINT of the evaluate.
```

```
Hi Patrick,

regarding the 32-bit numeric values that being passed to a win32 API
function:

according to the win32 API's rules,  if a numeric parameter is been
Passed to
a win32 API function --- then it should be formatted as by value, and
if a numeric
parameter is been Received from a win32 API function --- then it should
be
formatted as by reference.  However, this rule is not appling to MY own
entry-point
functions simply because,  when I am passing a numeric value to my
entry-point, the entry-point Per se is receiving a numeric value,
therefore I donot know if it
should be formatted as by reference or by value ? Usually when its
formatted wrong
the runtime system gives an error # 114 as I have mentioned above. I
was hoping
to find a frame-of-reference or a standard guideline as to how to pass
and receive
a 32-bit numeric values to MY own entry-point function.

thanks a lot for your reply and help.

kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** patrick.magee@microfocus.com
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T05:28:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108387710.547624.164170@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108137564.588728.163730@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <1108137564.588728.163730@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Passing and Receiving parameters
If you pass a 32 bit numeric by value, then it must be received by
value.
If you pass a 32 bit numeric by reference, then it must be received by
reference.
Depending on the complexity involved it is simplest to pass a numeric
value by value, and receive an additional value in return.
.... call 'EnrtyPoint' using by value aNumber returning anotherNumber
This way you have retained the original number and received an
additional number to be processed.
Handing values off to another program by value ensures that the other
program cannot corrupt your copy of the data.
Handing data off to another program by reference opens access to your
memory to the other program. This is usually done with some kind of
structure where you have assembled data for the other program to
process. Included should be some kind of indicator that the other
program can use to identify problems that exist with the data you
provided. Since you assembled the data you can recover the original
values and reverify its accuracy.



Kellie Fitton wrote:
> Hi Patrick,

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

should

own

> functions simply because,  when I am passing a numeric value to my
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

pass
> and receive
> a 32-bit numeric values to MY own entry-point function.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Hi Patrick,

you are 100% Correct.  However,  the NetExpress compiler only allows
these
standard rules based on the windows's messages that the program
receives in
windows's procedure section.  Case in point:

with WM-CREATE message
it is ok to use "by value" for a 32-bit numeric number

with WM-PAINT, WM-COMMAND, WM-CONTEXTMENU messages
it is NOT ok to use "by value" for a 32-bit numeric number, Why??
the compiler wants it to be "by reference"  ---  otherwise if I use "by
value" format,
I would get a runtime error # 114 as I have indicated before. So, I
usually reverse
it back to the opposite format, and the error message will disappear.
Weird right?

thanks a lot for your kind help and efforts.  Kellie.
```

- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108126996.811860.131430@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <1108137564.588728.163730@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1108387710.547624.164170@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108411087.473120.269050@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Kellie,
I'm not certain that I fully appreciate what you are describing, but I
can think of one point of confusion that may have some bearing. I
believe that all the constants you note are 16 bit values. Depending on
which Micro Focus compiler you are using these constants will be
processed differently. Older versions will interpret a 16 bit constant
as two bytes and pass only two bytes. You can override this behavior by
using a size specification, by value WM-CREATE size 4. The current
version will default to making all numeric constants into 32 bit
constants, in which case you might need to code, by value WM-CREATE
size 2. It all depends upon exactly what the function you are calling
requires.
Thanks, -magee

Kellie Fitton wrote:
> Hi Patrick,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the compiler wants it to be "by reference"  ---  otherwise if I use
"by
> value" format,
> I would get a runtime error # 114 as I have indicated before. So, I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help and efforts.  Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-11T14:50:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108162234.793885.279800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
> linkage section.
> 01 lnk-hWnd        hWnd.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 0001-main.
   ...............
> entry "MyWndProc" WinAPI using
>                  by value lnk-hwnd
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>          end-call

Note that there is only _one_ copy of each linkage section item when
using entry.  If you CALL .. using by reference lnk-hwnd and have this
as the entry using parameter then the original value changes and
becomes a pointer to its original value.

If this doesn't wind up with a complete corruption then returning back
to the call under WM-PAINT and using the resulting lnk-hWnd there will
certainly be wrong.

When you call an external program with its own linkage section the
items there are entirely distinct from the callers.  When you are
calling an entry there is no separate set of linkage items.  You can of
course set up another set yourself so that the ones used in the
callback are not corrupted by the call.  Or alternately just do a CALL
with no USING and the code in the ENTRY can just use the parameters in
linkage because they have been set up by the callback.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-12T00:36:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ccPd.1863091$f47.326496@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1107983060.681493.149260@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1108162234.793885.279800@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
Check out the (Micro Focus) "STICKY-LINKAGE" compiler directive for how to 
"impact" (neither very friendly IMHO) what happens to "addressability" to 
Linkage Section items  when you use ENTRY statements.

Bottom-Line (VERY personal opinion)

1) NEVER use ENTRY statements. (They aren't standard; they aren't portable; and, 
in general, they may produce "unpredictable" results)

2) DO use Nested programs (with an EVALUATE if you need it - in the main 
program) when "tempted" to use ENTRY Statements

3) If you absolutely POSITIVELY must use an ENTRY point, make certain that EVERY 
entry statement within the same program includes ALL the Linkage Section items 
that appear in ANY entry statement within the program (and of course make 
certain that you pass all the parameters when accessing each ENTRY point from an 
outside program

4) Besides what may happen to addressability with ENTRY points, make certain 
that you also understand what may happen with implicit or explicit CANCEL 
statements for program with ENTRY points. (Check out documentation for "DYNAM" 
and "NODYNAM" - true for both Micro Focus and IBM)

and can I say again,

PLEASE,
   don't use ENTRY statements !!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can cobol be framed-up legally?...

- **Date:** 2005-02-12T00:49:37-08:00

## Thread: Read and Write unknown record length? MVS — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Read and Write unknown record length? MVS

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T23:00:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Environment: IBM Mainframe, MVS.

How do I read a fixed length file of unknown record length into a data area
sized to the maximum expected record length? It would appear from my
research that I need to code RECORDING MODE IS U and possibly RECORD
CONTAINS 0. But, quoting from COBOL MVS & VM V1R2M2 Programming Guide: "If
you use the RECORD CONTAINS integer clause (RECORD Clause Format 1), the
record length is the integer value, regardless of the lengths of the
level-01 record description entries associated with the file." Surely that
statement is not true when integer equals 0, but I can find no other
description for that case.

If I can get a successful read, is there any way to determine what the
actual record length was? Can any of the experts give me the FD and READ
statements to handle this?

How about a simple program that reads a file of unknown record length (up
to some maximun of, say, 3000 bytes) and writes an exact copy, using the
record length from the input file. Can you include the JCL DD statements
for input and output files?
```

#### ↳ Re: Read and Write unknown record length? MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T23:11:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aMwOd.1643162$f47.283144@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
COBOL (IBM or otherwise - unless using some specific operating system 
"extensions") is NOT very good at reading files for which you do NOT know the 
"fixed file attributes" at compile-time.  (See file status = 39).

It sounds to me as if you might be better

A) using some IBM utility to do what you want

B) using ISPF library services (which can be accessed from within a COBOL 
program).  See:
:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ISPZSG30/1.5.3

and related information - for example,
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ispzsg30/1.3.3.9
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read and Write unknown record length? MVS

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T18:11:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<leWdnQ5A5Y9xX5ffRVn-vg@adelphia.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<BfxOd.29286$8H2.1003@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <aMwOd.1643162$f47.283144@news.easynews.com> <BfxOd.29286$8H2.1003@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
You _could_ write a program for the IBM mainframe which uses RECORD 0 (I 
have done so), but that is dependent on the operating system and 
compiler you are using.  It isn't part of the ANSI / ISO COBOL spec, but 
rather an IBM extension.  The operating system supplies the record size 
(LRECL) to the Data Control Block (DCB) at open time, if the program 
leaves it unspecified.  (Sorry, but I do not recall the details of how I 
got my program to process different record sizes.)

You say that your real target is a Windows op sys, not z/OS (or OS/390, 
or MVS).  I have never tried to write such a program for the PC, and I 
have old MF Language Reference manuals that do not seem to allow the use 
of "0" as the integer.  I don't think Windows is going to provide you 
with a record size, either.

In addition to these problems, your data may or may not be like it was 
on the mainframe.  You could use MF utilities to create what MF calls an 
ORGANIZATION RECORD SEQUENTIAL file, or the data might get downloaded 
such that it becomes ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL (delimited by the 
CR-LF characters).

You may want to look at what is being done in IEBPTPCH (some sort of 
rather simple data transformation, I expect), and add code to the COBOL 
programs which write the file that becomes input to IEBPTPCH, to 
duplicate what the utility is doing.  That is, when you are ready to 
write the existing output file, call a subroutine that writes the output 
that IEBPTPCH would write back on the host.  This would be more 
efficient, since you are working with the records in memory.

Or, if you don't want to touch the COBOL programs, you could write a 
series of simple new COBOL programs that duplicate the IEBPTPCH 
functions (record selection, reformatting, etc.)
====
William Bub wrote:

>Thanks. That wasn't too helpful    :)  (but probably accurate). Actually, I
>was trying to write a stripped version of IEBPTPCH in COBOL, to run on a
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>
```

```
<snip>
> Or, if you don't want to touch the COBOL programs, you could write a
> series of simple new COBOL programs that duplicate the IEBPTPCH
> functions (record selection, reformatting, etc.)

This is what I'm trying to do, but IEBPTPCH will use any record length. The
MicroFocus we are using is supposed to emulate the mainframe, and seems to
do it quite well. It has emulaters for DFSORT, IEBGENER, ICKEFT01, etc. (but
not IEBPTPCH). It uses a file catalog with all of the file attributes known
to the mainframe. And it runs JCL and expands PROCs like MVS does. My hope
was to write a program called IEBPTPCH so that we could run (for testing)
with the same JCL and PROCs already in use on the mainframe.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read and Write unknown record length? MVS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T05:34:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TnCOd.1671074$f47.288029@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <aMwOd.1643162$f47.283144@news.easynews.com> <BfxOd.29286$8H2.1003@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <leWdnQ5A5Y9xX5ffRVn-vg@adelphia.com> <sSzOd.29348$8H2.4855@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
As long as your target is Micro Focus and *NOT* IBM, then you can work with 
"undefined" record lengths (if you really want to do this yourself in COBOL).

Check out information on the FCDREG  compiler option and accessing the FCD 
(dynamically) from within your Micro Focus program.

I found information on it in

"File Handling > 7. Callable File Handler and Callable Sort APIs" in the Net 
Express 4.0 documentation (available online)

Again, if you code using this, it work with Micro Focus but NOT on the IBM 
mainframe.  (On the mainframe, you CAN accesss the DCB of a QSAM file by doing a 
CALL using FD-name but this won't really provide what I think you want)

Alternatively. contact Micro Focus - MFEEE already includes IDCAMS and IEBDG 
support, so they MIGHT be interested in providing PTPCH support as well.
```

```
William B, (as opposed to Bill K).

If you follow the above and DO it on a PC, Micro Focus, (back in DOS 
days), authored a Byte Stream file program. This allows you to read a 
file in 'chunks', or subject to memory the whole file. Apart from the 
ability to read in, then you are on your own to browse the input data 
and determine what you want to do with it.

If you want to give this a 'suck it and see' - e-mail me, ('editing' my 
address), and I'll send two versions from M/F which are Byteio.cbl and 
cblwritefile.cbl. These examples expand on the text of "Chapter 7 - File 
Handling", mentioned by Bill K. above.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Have you looked at the Mainframe Express, Enterprise Edition LRM under 
"Recording Mode"?
  (http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/mx30/mx30indx.htm)

It clearly (?) states for RECORDING MODE phrase

  "The "U" option is documentary only. "

P.S.  My "usually reliable" source at MF (like Jimmy in this forum) suggested 
that you post your question in the Micro Focus forum (AnswerLab) and see if 
anyone else has an IEBPTPCH solution.

Although I suspect that MF would take "enhancement requests" for missing IBM 
compatibility issues, there is certainly NO claim that MFEEE is currently 
*identical* to the IBM mainframe.  (For example, also in FD, BLOCK CONTAINS is 
also documentary)
```

#### ↳ Re: Read and Write unknown record length? MVS

- **From:** "Steve.T" <steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T18:56:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108004172.940910.47560@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<RBwOd.29278$8H2.27506@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
As was said by Mssrs Klein & Campbell, you are trying to do something
that COBOL is really not good at. I could go into a long explanation of
how the IBM utilities are written (in ALC or PL/X or PL/AS or PL/S),
how disk I/O differs between FBA & CKD, etc. ad nausem.

What you want to do is kind of emulate the behavior of the IBM utility.
Depending on what the real function is you are after you may be able to
use DOS commands (PRINT and COPY) to achieve the same results.

Regards,
Steve.T
```

## Thread: COMPUTE with negative value and unsigned destination — 4 msgs

### COMPUTE with negative value and unsigned destination

- **From:** "Walter Murray" <wmurray@midtown.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-09T23:16:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<420b0adc$1_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
2005-02-09T23:06-08:00

What does COBOL 85 (or COBOL 2002 for that matter) say about the behavior of
this program?

-----cut here-----
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. COBTEST.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  A   PIC 99      VALUE 23.
 01  B   PIC S99     VALUE -7.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 BEGIN.
     COMPUTE A = B
         ON SIZE ERROR     DISPLAY "SIZE ERROR"
         NOT ON SIZE ERROR DISPLAY "NOT SIZE ERROR"
     END-COMPUTE
     DISPLAY "A = " A
     STOP RUN.
 END PROGRAM COBTEST.
-----cut here-----

As I read the standard, a size error condition does not exist.  However, I
can't find anything that says what happens when a negative value is assigned
to an unsigned numeric data item.  With a MOVE statement, it's very clear
that the absolute value is stored.  I can't find where it says that for
COMPUTE.

The compiler I use displays "NOT SIZE ERROR" and "A = 07".  Does the
standard actually require that?  Where?

Walter



----== Posted via Newsfeeds.Com - Unlimited-Uncensored-Secure Usenet News==----
http://www.newsfeeds.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! 120,000+ Newsgroups
----= East and West-Coast Server Farms - Total Privacy via Encryption =----
```

#### ↳ Re: COMPUTE with negative value and unsigned destination

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T16:32:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B0MOd.1447379$B07.212035@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<420b0adc$1_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
For the '02 Standard, check out

A) Rule 4b on page 402
       and
B) GR(1) on page 431

To me, these mean that one does a "store" (move) and just use the absolute 
value.  However, I won't swear to this - and certainly won't swear it works this 
way in the '85 Standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMPUTE with negative value and unsigned destination

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@SpamStopper.Org>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T21:17:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lrqdnVsaWvFVvpHfRVn_vA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<420b0adc$1_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
> What does COBOL 85 (or COBOL 2002 for that matter) say about the behavior of
> this program?
>
> -----cut here-----

Since programming in the 70's I've always understood that data is stored
according to the format of the receiving field. Since A is PIC 99 no sign can
be stored therefore A will = 07. This is a very common defect in many programs
that I've repaired innumerable times when users have complained that their reports
"don't add up".

Editorial Comment: Always, always, always use S on the picture clause of every
numeric field that can possibly be used in a calculation or that represents a numeric
quantity. Always, have a sign on the picture clause of every numeric field that prints.
Never mind that you're told the field "won't be negative". Someday it will be negative,
and you'll save yourself hours of headaches trying to find the lone negative quantity
that's screwing up your report or file. The only exception should be data elements that
logically cannot be negative, like a phone number or a date.
```

#### ↳ Re: Typical SCCSID string in a Cobol source on Unix platform

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T10:08:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108058907.440647.223340@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<420b6a5a_3@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<420b6a5a_3@127.0.0.1>`

```
> how to declare a "static char - like in C language"

In C 'static' is used for several things.  Inside a function it makes a
declaration non-dynamic.  Outside a function, where everything is
non-dynamic, it makes it 'file local'.

> static char SCCSId = "@(#) Progname - Version ID - ModificationDate";
> and the what command returns :
> "Progname - Version ID - ModificationDate".

I don't know if 'what' is a general Unix command or something written
in house, but it seems that it simply searches through the specified
file looking for '@(#)' and then outputs up to the next null
terminator.  I have written something similar myself for the same
purpose.

The only point of being 'static' is that if modules are linked together
there is not a clash in having the same variable name.

In Cobol you would just need to have a WORKING-STORAGE variable with
the required content followed by a null terminator:

    WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    01  SCCSId.
         03 FILLER PIC X(nn) VALUE
              "@(#) programname ....".
         03 FILLER PIC X VALUE LOW-VALUE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Typical SCCSID string in a Cobol source on Unix platform

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T18:13:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvNOd.355784$Xk.14495@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<420b6a5a_3@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<420b6a5a_3@127.0.0.1>`

```
MORANDT wrote:
> Hi,
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> In Cobol Language : how can I do ?

A fairly big topic to describe in full detail - depending upon your 
COBOL compiler, look at on-line LRMs (Language Reference Manuals) - 
words like PICTURE, INITIALIZE, LITERALS, CONSTANTS, REDEFINES, use of 
WORKING-STORAGE etc. Go to vendor sites and look for coded examples. 
They wont specifically cover the above topics, but you will see the 
techniques tucked away in programs describing how to use other features.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

*> Note - double quotes
01 Text1	pic x(31) "This text is the size specified".

*> Note - single quotes
01 Text2        pic x(50) 'This text is less than size specified'.

*> Numerics
01 Text3	pic 9(03) value 500.
01 Text4	pic 9(03)v9(02) value 123.45.

*> CONSTANTS - Part of new COBOL 2002 Standard, but as yet not
*> implemented by any vendor. However, Micro Focus compilers have a 
current extension for CONSTANTS :-

78 Text5	value "Hello World".
78 Text6	value 1.234.
78 Text7	value 2000.

*> No initial values - so be sure to INITIALIZE before using them !!!!! 
Alternatively, give them initial values as shown

01 Text8	pic x(40) value spaces.
01 Text9        pic 9(03) value zeroes.

move 'Good morning' to Text8
move 23 to Text9

and, your own example :-

01 SCCSId  pic x(40) value "Progname - Version ID - ModificationDate".


Above is in no way exhaustive - but it's a start :-)


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Typical SCCSID string in a Cobol source on Unix platform

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-10T18:16:07
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cug7hq$46m$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<420b6a5a_3@127.0.0.1>`

```
Hi.

Something along the lines of the following will do the trick :

        78 Progname         VALUE "Progname".
        78 VerID            VALUE "VerID".
        78 ModificationDate VALUE "ModificationDate".
        01 SCCSId pic x(60) value x"0D0A" & "@(#)"
            & Progname & " - " & VerID & " - " & ModificationDate
            & x"0D0A".

where you could pass the level 78 items -- constants -- in at compile-time.

Or, more simply, use :

        01 SCCSId pic x(60)
           value x"0D0A" & "@(#)My What String" & x"0D0A".

SimonT.
```

```
Montgomery County Public Schools (MCPS), Rockville, MD, provides 137,000
students a world-class education. A stable, collegial, professional
workforce applies
cutting-edge technology to support classrooms and offices in a suburban
Washington, DC community rich in educational, cultural and social
opportunities. Our mission provides a wonderful opportunity to make a
difference.

MCPS provides excellent benefits, which is comprised of the following
components: medical, dental, vision, prescription drug coverage, a
retirement plan, flexible spending accounts, tax-deferred annuity (TDA)
403(b) and 457 plans, long term health care and life. Please e-mail cover
letter and resume to: Mrs. Dale S. Horos: Dale_Horos@mcpsmd.org



Application Developer II
Candidate will provide application development and support for the Lawson
HR suite.

Candidate must have:
� Minimum of five years of software development experience.
� Program design and development skills using COBOL or MicroFocus COBOL.
� Strong technical and functional knowledge of business applications
� Bachelor degree from an accredited college or university.

Other skills desired and considered:
� Experience with the support or Lawson business application products
including writing custom programs using Lawson Cobol.
� Experience in developing web base applications
� JAVAScript, HTML, Lawson Design Studio, SQL
� Knowledge of C#, .Net and Rational UML
� Report Writers such as Crystal or Discoverer

The person in this position will work as a lead application development
expert working with end users, programmers and other team members to
develop solutions to business and technical requirements. Duties include
requirement collection, development of complex functional and technical
specifications, analysis, design, coding, testing, implementation and
technical support. This position is also expected to direct and mentor
other programmers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-13T16:48:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com>`

```
>> Pls post  your time and PC speed..

If the 'winner' is the fastest then the uniqueness mechanism chosen is
likely to be more influential than the language it is coded in.

 > total words    = 789781
> unique words =  12691

This indicates that on average there will be 64 or so lookups for each
word.  The program will only execute the 'new word found' code 12691
times wheras it will do the 'is this unique' 789781 times.

Short words are more likely to have a very high frequency.

For example the program may be coded as a simple array or as a pointer
list and the time will be long because of the search time.

It could be done faster using a binary or n-way tree in an array or a
dynamic structure.

Or it could be done using a hash table (with overflow).

I would guess that a 27-way tree could be fastest.  An array of many
thousand 'usage index occurs 27' items. The 27th represents end-word.
This would probably need to be dynamic.

This should mean that a word can be determined as alreay seen in n
double index hits where word size is n.

The absolute fastest would be using a hash using all letters that will
generate a unique hash.  This would require a gigabyte of RAM if each
word set a bit and only 7 letter words were considered.

Perhaps a combination mechanism.  For words of 4 letters or less use a
hash into a half megabyte array of bytes.  For longer words use the
27-way b-tree calculating each 2nd index from the letter.

As they say, a QuickSort in interpreted BASIC is likely to outperform a
hand coded assembler bubble sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T10:16:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108404971.639586.104170@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9Qs5Ar29flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <9Qs5Ar29flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
>> I would guess that a 27-way tree could be fastest.  An array of many
>> thousand 'usage index occurs 27' items. The 27th represents
end-word.

> I do not really understand what you mean.

It is necessary to identify quickly whether a word has already been
added to the structure. By converting a word into a series of numeric
values a->1, b->2 etc, endword->27 we can use these values as indexes:

       01  word-check.
             03  Word-letter         PIC 99 COMP-5 OCCURS 30.

       01  WA-Limit                  PIC 9(8) COMP-5 VALUE 1.
       01  WA-Index                 PIC 9(8) COMP-5.
       01  Word-array.
             03  WA-item            OCCURS 100000.
               05 WA-letter          PIC 9(8) COMP-5 OCCURS 27.

MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES   TO Word-array

using word 'adam' -> values in word-letter(n)  of  1, 4, 1, 13, 27, 0,
...

approximately and untested:

|    MOVE 1    TO WA-Index
|    PERFORM varying n from 1 by 1 until word-letter(n) = ZERO
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
|    end-perform

Note that 'add-word' will be called several times for each actual new
word so it needs to only count 1 and then 'switch off' until the next
word is tried.

The 'end word' value of 27 is required so that the code can distinguish
between 'adam' and 'adams'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T11:09:58-06:00

```
Richard wrote:
> 
> >> Pls post  your time and PC speed..
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

The 'easiest' way to do the counting is to do it as a SORT: in the input
procedure extract all the words and release them; in the output
procedure just return them and do the counts.  This is (or was, anyway)
the standard method for concordances and word counts.  I'd be interested
to hear of comparative results for sorting vs. in-memory tables.

I find it mildly alarming that the text of the Bible is posted on
"patriot.net": the implication is disturbing.

PL
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T21:27:00+00:00

- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  14.02.05
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


WMK> If someone else wants to "tune" it further by converting my INSPECT
WMK> and UNSTRING statements into "PERFORM loops" - I would
WMK> (personally) be interested in what difference that would make
WMK> (with different compilers and O/S)

   well, I do not really want to write a program myself (actually I  
should better work on my customer project instead of reading  
Netnews...), but your "parse-inp" section would look different ...


    Rather something like this:

--------- schnipp -----------------------------------------

  READ input-file
  PERFORM UNTIL NOT has-reached-EOF IN input-file-status
    MOVE = TO Pointer-Char-in-Line
    *> have a variable containing the line length ...
    PERFORM UNTIL Pointer-Char-in-Line >= length-of-Line
      *> skipping over eventual leading spaces
      INSPECT text-line
          TALLYING Pointer-Char-in-Line FOR LEADING SPACES
      ADD 1 TO Pointer-Char-in-Line
      MOVE ZERO TO word-length
      PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
          UNTIL delimiter-found NOT = "'"
             OR word-length = 0
        UNSTRING text-line
            DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
                         OR "." OR "," OR ":" OR ";" OR "-" OR "'"
                         OR "?" OR "!" OR "(" OR ")" OR '"'
            INTO word-found(word-length - 1:)
              DELIMITER IN delimiter-found
              COUNT IN word-length
            WITH POINTER Pointer-Char-in-Line
            TALLYING IN word-count-per-line
      END-PERFORM  *> skipping over apostrophs
      IF  word-length = 0
        OR word-found NUMERIC
      THEN
        SUBTRACT 1 FROM word-count-per-line
        *> is the above line really necessary? Otherwise: CONTINUE
      ELSE
        PERFORM process-word-found
      END-IF
    END-PERFORM  *> finding individual words in one line
    READ input-file
  END-PERFORM  *> processing lines read and fetch the next line


------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

  I have just typed this in the newsreader, and have neither checked  
the syntax nor some questions like if I have to increment the char- 
pointer after each UNSTRING

  The two main differentiating ideas compared to yours is that this  
code looks at each character of the input line once, and that there is  
not a table of words per line, but the words are being extracted and  
processed word by word.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Belehrung findet man ï¿½fter in der Welt als Trost.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T21:46:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9QsCOqvPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <9QsBrdneflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
.    On  14.02.05
  wrote  L.Willms@JPBERLIN.de (Lueko Willms)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  9QsBrdneflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


LW>   PERFORM UNTIL NOT has-reached-EOF IN input-file-status
LW>     MOVE = TO Pointer-Char-in-Line

   oops, the shift key did not release quickly enough ...
   the above line should read

        MOVE 0 TO Pointer-Char-in-Line

   or better

        MOVE ZERO TO Pointer-Char-in-Line


LW>     *> have a variable containing the line length ...
LW>     PERFORM UNTIL Pointer-Char-in-Line >= length-of-Line
LW>       *> skipping over eventual leading spaces
LW>       INSPECT text-line
LW>           TALLYING Pointer-Char-in-Line FOR LEADING SPACES
LW>       ADD 1 TO Pointer-Char-in-Line
LW>       MOVE ZERO TO word-length


   that 0 and = are on the same key may not be obvious to people using  
an english keyboard...

   And in the loop to put together words from parts to the left and to  
the right of an apostroph:


LW>         UNSTRING text-line
LW>             DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
LW>                          OR "." OR "," OR ":" OR ";" OR "-" OR "'"
LW>                          OR "?" OR "!" OR "(" OR ")" OR '"'
LW>             INTO word-found(word-length - 1:)

   there should be a + (plus) sign in the reference modification, not  
a minus (-) sign. Not so much a typing error, but an eror to forget  
what has been thought out ... after writing:

LW>       MOVE ZERO TO word-length

   ts, ts, ts


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Man stattete ihm sehr heiï¿½en, schon etwas verbrannten Dank ab. - 
G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
me to be able to "excercise" my COBOL skills - for something other than a 
10-line "how do you do this" type of test program.

More seriously, thanks for your post.  I'll look at the code in a little more 
length.  I was actually thinking of doing a PERFORM loop thru the input lines 
without ANY inspect (or unstring) but just "parsing" each word at a time.  I may 
(or may not) still try and do this.
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> For those interested in a NEW version of my program that:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference that would make (with different compilers and O/S)

I played with this and played with this - an array with "SEARCH" is 
*not* the best COBOL way to solve this challenge.  :)

Using Perform loops with character-by-character moves using indexed 
tables, it took about 30 minutes.  Changed the actual moves to an 
UNSTRING, and it took 25 minutes.  Of course, that doesn't account for 
additional load on the machine, but we've got teeny weeny machines for 
our development platforms...
```

```
Just out of curiosity, did you try MY "version 2" program.  If it was what took 
20 minutes, that would really surprise me (on any O/S - with any compiler).

Obviously, change the Select/Assign clause (and get rid of the initial @OPTIONS 
statement) but other than that, I *think* my code should work with any '85/'89 
Standard compiler. (It could be changed to work with an '85 Standard compiler, 
but I used a couple of intrinsic functions for "easy" stuff like CURRENT-DATE 
and UPPER-CASE - the former could be done with an ACCEPT and the later with an 
INSPECT CONVERTING)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T19:46:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
On Thu, 17 Feb 2005 11:26:22 -0600, Peter Lacey
<lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:


>Based on what I've seen posted so far, this is the speed winner ... and
>is very much easier to understand than some of the programs posted.

How sad that a program inserting 'records' into an ISAM file is the
speed winner. Demos I posted a few months ago showed that approach to
be 15 times slower than a single-threaded in-memory approach. They
also showed an OO solution via Micro Focus' Collection Class to be 60
times slower. 

We're making negative 'progress'. What's next? A database solution?

The correct answer is parallel processing via multithreading.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T18:20:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com>`

```
> How sad that a program inserting 'records' into an ISAM file is the
> speed winner.

It may make you sad, but it may be an appropriate solution.

> Demos I posted a few months ago showed that approach to
> be 15 times slower than a single-threaded in-memory approach.

No. Wrong.  Your demo did show some selected operations to run faster
than others. They did not show that this the mechanisms in the demo
were relevant to _this_ problem.

> They also showed an OO solution via Micro Focus' Collection Class to
be 60
> times slower.

Irrelevant.

> We're making negative 'progress'. What's next? A database solution?

A database solution may be entirely suitable, depending on what real
world problems was actually being solved.

> The correct answer is parallel processing via multithreading.

Please show us, Robert, show us how you will parallel process by
starting 8 million threads, one for each word.

In fact 'multithreading' is not an answer at all, let alone a 'correct'
one.  You need to show an algorithm that will solve the problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T04:12:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 17 Feb 2005 18:20:02 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Demos I posted a few months ago showed that approach to
>> be 15 times slower than a single-threaded in-memory approach.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>were relevant to _this_ problem.

The demos took a large number of distinct random keys, looked for and
inserted them into a collection, then retrieved them. Unlike this
case, there were no duplicates; otherwise the problem was the same.

>> They also showed an OO solution via Micro Focus' Collection Class to
>be 60
>> times slower.
>
>Irrelevant.

OO is touted as the modern solution.

>> We're making negative 'progress'. What's next? A database solution?
>
>A database solution may be entirely suitable, depending on what real
>world problems was actually being solved.

I have no problem abstracting real world problems from lists of unique
random numbers or duplicated bible words. 

>> The correct answer is parallel processing via multithreading.
>
>Please show us, Robert, show us how you will parallel process by
>starting 8 million threads, one for each word.

The parser is single-threaded. Having parsed a word, it can launch a
thread per word to lookup and insert, without waiting for completion
before parsing the next input word.

>In fact 'multithreading' is not an answer at all, let alone a 'correct'
>one.  You need to show an algorithm that will solve the problem.

I just described it. Did the others post timing? If they did, a
parallel solution will blow them away by doing it  (.75 * number of
CPUs) times faster.  Adding the inefficiency of a file system, the
difference with only four processors will be on the order of 100 times
faster.

Parallel processing isn't a technical trick suitable to a narrow class
of problems; it's the whole future of computing. Ten years hence it
will be the norm.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T20:40:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108701600.337896.265630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com>`

```
> parallel solution will blow them away by doing it  (.75 * number of
CPUs) times faster.

So for a single CPU it will run at 75% the speed ( 1.33 the time) of a
non-multithread solution.

> Ten years hence it will be the norm.

Maybe.  In the meantime I'll continue using my single CPU desktop
machine which typically is 98% idle waiting for keystrokes. I am sure
that the next range of machines from IBM will use cell processors, I'll
get one when I think that I need to have a 99.8% idle time.

> The parser is single-threaded. Having parsed a word, it can launch a
> thread per word to lookup and insert, without waiting for completion
> before parsing the next input

While it may well be that there could be two threads, one to parse and
put into a queue, and one to pull words from the queue and maintain a
structure of words, any attempt to do it 'thread per word' would be a
complete disaster that would require to spend more time spinning on a
lock than doing actual work.

> there were no duplicates; otherwise the problem was the same.

That actually does make it a different problem.  For example two
threads have the same word and discover it doesn't exist.  Both decide
it needs to be inserted, solution fails.

> I just described it.

No. Wrong. You just described something that _doesn't_ work.


>> They also showed an OO solution via Micro Focus' Collection Class to
>> be 60 times slower.

> OO is touted as the modern solution.

How is your rewrite of this class progressing ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-19T04:06:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0vad11hnfse2tv2v1hglfnjhs55qe6qgub@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com> <1108701600.337896.265630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 17 Feb 2005 20:40:00 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> parallel solution will blow them away by doing it  (.75 * number of
>CPUs) times faster.
>
>So for a single CPU it will run at 75% the speed ( 1.33 the time) of a
>non-multithread solution.

True, if you use a multi-threading runtime to execute a
non-multi-threading program.

>> Ten years hence it will be the norm.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>get one when I think that I need to have a 99.8% idle time.

If you wait until multi-threading is on every desktop, you'll be too
late. Developers should target future machines.

>> The parser is single-threaded. Having parsed a word, it can launch a
>> thread per word to lookup and insert, without waiting for completion
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>lock than doing actual work.

Wrong. Assuming a lock per leaf rather than a single lock on the tree,
the program would wait only when two words try to update the same
leaf. There would be a thread per word plus one for the parser, not
two threads.

>> there were no duplicates; otherwise the problem was the same.
>
>That actually does make it a different problem.  For example two
>threads have the same word and discover it doesn't exist.  Both decide
>it needs to be inserted, solution fails.

It's the same with two threads inserting different random numbers.
Unless they happen to be on the same leaf, there's no contention.

>> I just described it.
>
>No. Wrong. You just described something that _doesn't_ work.

It may not work in your mind but my posted demo works on all
computers. Isn't reality great for cancelling subjectivity?

>>> They also showed an OO solution via Micro Focus' Collection Class to
>>> be 60 times slower.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>How is your rewrite of this class progressing ?

I got the name of the Micro Focus author and a strong prompt to write
to him, from a former colleague. Do you think he'd welcome criticism
or be grateful for a better solution?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T20:31:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108787503.329008.64020@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0vad11hnfse2tv2v1hglfnjhs55qe6qgub@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com> <P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com> <1108701600.337896.265630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <0vad11hnfse2tv2v1hglfnjhs55qe6qgub@4ax.com>`

```
>>> parallel solution will blow them away by doing it  (.75 * number of
>>>CPUs) times faster.
>> So for a single CPU it will run at 75% the speed ( 1.33 the time) of
a
>> non-multithread solution.

> True, if you use a multi-threading runtime to execute a
> non-multi-threading program.

Or if the machine only has a single CPU, or if the machine is running
other processes such that only one CPU is allocated to your program.

You seem to imagine that you will have exclusive use of a multi-CPU
machine.  Even your timings were done (IIRC) by shutting down all other
services.

> Wrong. Assuming a lock per leaf rather than a single lock on the
tree,
> the program would wait only when two words try to update the same
> leaf. There would be a thread per word plus one for the parser, not
> two threads

So now you increase the code bloat by having a thread lock check at
each node. You will spending more time in code execution than 3 CPUs
will ever recover.

Now there are good reasons for using multi-threading, this just isn't
one of them.

> It's the same with two threads inserting different random numbers.
> Unless they happen to be on the same leaf, there's no contention.

But you still have to write code to ensure that checks for contention,
code that bloats out and doubles the execution time (in this particular
case).

> It may not work in your mind

It doesn't work for the posted problem because it doesn't handle
duplicates and the resulting comtention.

> but my posted demo works on all computers.

But slower on single CPU machines than a single-threaded solution.

> Isn't reality great for cancelling subjectivity?

You always seem to be in a different reality than the rest of the
world.

> Do you think he'd welcome criticism or be grateful for a better
solution?

So far you've only offered criticism, do come back when you actually
have a 'better solution'.
```

```
contention,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-20T04:10:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<helg11dbj17d2h5ffpcro7eskb0v7bskqj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com> <1108701600.337896.265630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <0vad11hnfse2tv2v1hglfnjhs55qe6qgub@4ax.com> <1108787503.329008.64020@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <21of115rt5b36ceq42su2mpfuqp37n9hgf@4ax.com> <1108877540.850547.216850@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 19 Feb 2005 21:32:20 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>>> So now you increase the code bloat by having a thread lock check at
>>> each node. You will spending more time in code execution than 3 CPUs
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>discussion.

My code did lock tree nodes in common memory before writing to them. I
did it the offical way, then showed how to do it faster.  Comments
described the logic in some detail. 

-- begin quotation --
* When building a tree with parallel processing, we must lock forward
* pointers before updating them to prevent 'collisions' -- two threads
* updating the same pointer at the same time. This demo shows two
* techniques for doing that. Case 13 uses mutex (mutual exclusion),
the
* lowest level, fastest and universally available of the
synchronization
* tools in the threads library. The others, not used here, are
monitor,
* semaphore and event, which are functionally similar to mutex.
*
* A mutex is a 'token' that can have only one owner. This demo uses
* 50,000 mutexes, one per node. Before updating a node, it asks for
* exclusive ownership of the node's mutex. If ownership cannot be
* obtained, it asks the thread library to put the thread to sleep
until
* the mutex is available. Then it updates the node and releases the
* mutex.
*
* Case 14 uses a novel method of self-synchronization. Rather than
* calling a library function, it avoids collisions by simply
* manipulating flags in shared memory. It uses an array of busy
* indicators, one per thread, puts its quarter on the edge of the pool
* table, then counts the number of quarters up there. If the result is
* 1, it MAY be the exclusive owner. However, while it was counting
* ..5..6..7.. another thread may have put a quarter in slot 1.
Wouldn't
* thread 1 count 2 quarters and give up for awhile? Not necessarily.
* Thread 1 may be suspended during its counting. By the time it wakes
* up, this thread's quarter has been removed. To handle that case, the
* program checks whether the pointer is still zero after counting one
* stakeholder. It will see the pointer deposited by this thread and
* thereby avoid a collision.
*
* The speed difference between the two methods is dramatic. Case 13,
* mutex, took 5.9 seconds -- 3.3 times slower than single-threading.
* Case 14 took .6 seconds, 3 times faster than single-threading, on a
* machine with 4 CPUs. The mutex function overhead defeats
* multitasking's promise of higher speed due to parallel processing.
* However, self-synchronization delivers the expected performance.


>> Detecting duplicates doesn't cause contention.
>
>Yes it will.  It will cause contention when two threads try to update
>the same node to, for example, add the same word.

Duplicates are detected by by reading the tree, not by writing to it.
Reading requires no locks. The case of two threads trying to insert
the same new word at the same time is a rare exception. When you
assert duplicate detection requires locks, you're misleading the
reader.

>> Counting word frequency would cause it,
>
>That, too will cause contention.  Code will have to check for it and
>apply locks.

That's what I said. Detecting a duplicate requires no locks, but
adding 1 to a duplicate counter on each node requires locking the
node.

>> as it did in your Python solution.
>
>Explain please.  Or did you just completely not understand what the
>exception handling was doing.

When you added duplicate counting, your execution time went up 30%.

>> The resulting slowdown is relatively small.
>
>So you do agree that there will be 'slowdown' at least.

A gross count of duplicates doesn't slow the process. It can be done
in the single-threaded parser. Counting occurrences per word requires
a lockable counter on each node (word). I don't think the original
problem spec called for that. It appears to have been added later.
Your first Python solution didn't have it; your second did.
```

```
> Duplicates are detected by by reading the tree, not by writing to it.
> Reading requires no locks.

An if one thread is checking to see if a word exists while another
thread is about to insert it but hasn't got CPU time yet then it will
find it not there and will try to insert.  You will get duplicate
insertions.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> I got the name of the Micro Focus author and a strong prompt to write
> to him, from a former colleague. Do you think he'd welcome criticism
> or be grateful for a better solution?
> 
Robert,

Well I think you've got it wrong before you even start ! Nobody, but 
nobody welcomes blunt CRITICISM. Rethink what you wrote using the word 
SUGGESTIONS. Depending upon how it is written, (diplomacy isn't your 
greatest skill is it ?), the recipient, subject to their own 
personality, could be pissed off, or again subject to your wording, 
acknowledge your ideas - even if they don't get implemented.

Bearing in mind that the collection structure has been around since 
VISOC, which in turn may have been 'yanked' from their earlier Object 
COBOL Workbench, I'd be surprised if the individual was still around. He 
may be a 'loyalist' and still doing his thing. Assuming it was written 
at Newbury, as a result of the Merant cock-up a fair number of bright 
people left some years ago. Fortunately there are still a group of good 
guys around. (Your man may still be around).

Let's acknowledge Patrick Magee who has recently posted here. Been doing 
  sterling support since M/F Compuserve Forum days.

Then there's the problem of perhaps a bureaucratic hierarchy. (I don't 
know how Newbury is structured and whether or not all the programming is 
still done there). The man you want might be a 2nd Lieutenant and 
logically you should be posing your ideas at his boss, a Major or a Lt. Col.

Softly, softly, catchee monkey. I'd be inclined to write privately to 
Bill K. He could suggest to whom you might address your suggestions.

Another thing - don't look for an enhancement in N/E 4.0. Probably would 
appear in N/E 5.0 after they have had a 'shufti' at what the COBOL 
Standards gang have produced.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
On Tue, 22 Feb 2005 12:58:05 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:i5pf11tra6gjjono142n8auhc2g966143k@4ax.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>One man's "error" is another man's "carefully-considered design decision."

Yeahbut, the distinction between public and private is not
situational.

Common Sense  
Surprising new research shows that crowds are often smarter than
individuals  
By Michael Shermer  
 
 There is now overwhelming evidence, artfully accumulated and
articulated by New Yorker columnist James Surowiecki in his
enthralling 2004 book, The Wisdom of Crowds (Doubleday), that "the
many are smarter than the few." In one experiment, participants were
asked to estimate the number of jelly beans in a jar. The group
average was 871, only 2.5 percent off the actual figure of 850. Only
one of the 56 subjects was closer. The reason is that in a group,
individual errors on either side of the true figure cancel each other
out. 

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
For a group to be smart, it should be autonomous, decentralized and
diverse. 
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A similar result was discovered in an example so counterintuitive that
it startles. When the U.S. submarine Scorpion disappeared in May 1968,
a naval scientist named John Craven assembled a diverse group of
submarine experts, mathematicians and salvage divers. Instead of
putting them in a room to consult one another, he had each of them
give a best guesstimate--based on the sub's last known speed and
position (and nothing else)--of the cause of its demise and its rate
and steepness of descent, among other variables. Craven then computed
a group average employing Bayes's theorem, a statistical method
wherein a probability is assigned to each component of a problem. The
Scorpion's location on the ocean floor was only 220 yards from the
averaged prediction. 

 
Stranger still was the stock market's reaction on January 28, 1986,
the day the space shuttle Challenger exploded. Of the four major
shuttle contractors--Lockheed, Rockwell International, Martin Marietta
and Morton Thiokol--the last (the builder of the defective
solid-rocket booster) was hit hardest, with a 12 percent loss,
compared with only 3 percent for the others. A detailed study of the
market (a sizable crowd, indeed!) by economists Michael T. Maloney of
Clemson University and J. Harold Mulherin of Claremont McKenna College
could find no evidence of insider trading or media focus on the rocket
booster or on Morton Thiokol. Given four possibilities, the masses
voted correctly.  

http://www.sciam.com/article.cfm?chanID=sa006&colID=13&articleID=00049F3E-91E1-119B-8EA483414B7FFE9F
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<rqmn11hhcrkq0cimq61ahc7pbs6t65ldlp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<P07Qd.2162598$f47.379300@news.easynews.com> <4214D3BE.2B45AE71@mb.sympatico.ca> <arga119ci214i0sjrrks90tqj6h1occkec@4ax.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com> <1108701600.337896.265630@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <0vad11hnfse2tv2v1hglfnjhs55qe6qgub@4ax.com> <eCARd.430478$8l.312543@pd7tw1no> <i5pf11tra6gjjono142n8auhc2g966143k@4ax.com> <cvg6ct$2lsi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <rqmn11hhcrkq0cimq61ahc7pbs6t65ldlp@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
>  There is now overwhelming evidence, artfully accumulated and
> articulated by New Yorker columnist James Surowiecki in his
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Both those examples point to the exact opposite of your premise. In both 
cases, a large number of participants acted as individuals, NOT as a 
group. The groups quoted were not a "smart groups". The results were 
obtained by isolating individuals, ensuring the individuals did NOT act 
as a group, and extracting a result through statistics.

If your premise had been "For a group to be smart, it cannot act as a 
group, it must not be a group, and the members must be chosen based on 
their isolation from each other", then your examples may have been germaine.

Frankly, I do not really see anything unusual or new in the fact that 
people investing a lot in the defence industry know more than average 
about the defence industry, but maybe that's just me. To think it 
startling new knowledge and a counter-intuitive revelation of human 
nature seems kinda weird.

BTW, do you have an URL to the "contest" solution you posted?  What were 
your benchmarks?  I could not find them in here or on Google.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T08:04:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9R7h1kFPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108693202.508508.319200@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  18.02.05
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  r2pa11thn9j6kg3ekhs62ha6r2eqe04qb9@4ax.com
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


>> Please show us, Robert, show us how you will parallel process by
>> starting 8 million threads, one for each word.

RW> The parser is single-threaded. Having parsed a word, it can launch a
RW> thread per word to lookup and insert, without waiting for completion
RW> before parsing the next input word.

   Sure, but a) we were looking here for a COBOL solution, and not  
many COBOL compilers do support parallell processes (I did work with  
one, @ACOB from Unisys' OS/1100, implementing COBOL-74), and b) on a  
computer with one single CPU it is to be doubted if the saving in time  
by parallell threads is greater than the overhead of the process  
switching.

   Of course, if large amounts of time are spent by waiting for an I/ 
O-operation to complete, the saving may be substantial, but the  
parsing of a single word does not encompass I/O, only reading of the  
next line, which is probably done in larger buffers anyway.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Wir wohnen in Gï¿½ttingen in Scheiterhaufen, die mit Tï¿½ren und Fenstern versehen sind. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```
