- **Message-ID:** `<cur39a$r9o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <1108342116.221843.109250@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <4210DB66.B7BFC11F@mb.sympatico.ca> <nf6Qd.3780$U86.785@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
LX-i retorted:

<<Only to the paranoid...>>

Naah.

I too wonder about the association of the prominence of the promotion of the
King James Bible, without any reference to any other translation or any
questions about the 15th-to-17th-century scholarship that underlies it, with
the concept of "patriotism".   I have great respect for the King James
particularly as a scholarly translation of the Greek Text that underlies its
New Testament, but I would have to admit some alarm at the fact that the
Douay-Rheims that's relatively contemporary with it is nowhere mentioned on
this "patriot" website.

The only version of the Bible downloadable from this "patriotic" website is
the King James.

Are there any other versions of the Bible that can legitimately be accepted
as "patriotic"?  What about other writings people hold sacred, like the
Koran and the Book of Mormon?  What of those that reject the New Testament
outright?  What of the Apocrypha that Protestants reject and Catholics
accept?  Are those who accept such books as "inspired" alongside, or as
alternative to, the King James Bible somehow intrinsically less patriotic?
King James Bibles published for the first couple of centuries included the
Apocrypha; only much later were they deleted.  Should a "patriotic" word
count be based on a King James Bible that *includes* the Apocrypha, or must
one restrict oneself to one that does not?

I have to agree that the *implication* that the King James Bible (of any
vintage!) is THE patriotic Bible (implicit in its being so prominently
featured in a website that also prominently features the American flag)
differs only in degree from the attitude I read of in recent news, that
Group A is comprised of the TRUE followers of Mohammed and Group B is a
bunch of infidels that fundamentally deserve to be exterminated like
cockroaches.  It doesn't much matter to me which particular group is Group A
and Group B; my problem is with the fact that there is so much animosity and
contempt (veiled or otherwise) comingling politics with religion.

I have acquaintances that make a big point of Demonstrating their Patriotism
with a combination of American flags flying from their car's side windows
with really-insulting anti-Islam stickers on their bumpers.

I have run into the attitude that neither a Jew nor a Mormon can have
anything positive to offer society in business, in society or in government
precisely because they aren't True Believers.  Evidence of such attitudes
can be overt -- and I have encountered it explicitly and overtly in the
workplace -- but it can also be subtle, and it's subtle associations like
"King James Bible = Patriotic" that strike me, apparently along with Peter
Lacey, as "mildly alarming".

I had a co-worker inform me one time that his religion obligated him to haul
me out in front of the workplace and, along with all the other True
Believers to stone me to death on the front steps of our workplace; it was a
direct violation of the God-given Bill of Rights for the state to prevent
him from doing so.

I had a different co-worker inform me that his religion taught him that a
Jew cannot have any integrity in any context or circumstance unless and
until he or she accepts Christianity, and that therefore his religion
obligated him to ensure -- "by hook or by crook" -- to ensure that
management was not deluded into thinking that they did.

Perhaps you have not personally encountered such circumstances.  But I have,
and I have to agree with Peter's characterization of the association of
"King James Version" with "patriot" as "mildly alarming".

I mistrust individuals as well as groups that identify themselves or their
views with self-promoting adjectives like "patriotic", "humble", "expert",
"proper Christian" and so forth.

    -Chuck Stevens.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T21:57:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

On 14-Feb-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> Are there any other versions of the Bible that can legitimately be accepted
> as "patriotic"?

When someone is strong about "Patriotism" or "the Bible" (or the Koran), they
rarely are willing to accept that someone else with a slightly different concept
of these words is anything less than a Spawn of Satan.    When someone's worship
of Country or God is such that they are willing to tromp on what the country is
built upon, or the teachings of his God, then they are serving the enemies of
their country or religion.




The card game Illuminati had lots of groups trying to control the world.  Part
of the design was that there were opposite groups (Democrat vs Republican).  
Every group had its opposite except for one.   I think it was named "radicals"
and its opposite was itself.    I think this is an apt way of determining
radicalism.    If Sunnis hate Shiites more than they hate atheists, then they
are radical.   At times both Patriotism and Christianity have been there.   We
aren't quite there now - but moving in that direction.
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cur70q$b4n$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> At times both Patriotism and Christianity have been there.   We
> aren't quite there now - but moving in that direction.

I know lots of people -- and some groups -- who are indeed quite there now!

There's a group of Baptists in Kansas who are calling upon the world to
recognize that King Carl Gustav VI of Sweden is the "King of Sodomite
Whores"?  "Thank God for the tsunamis -- we hope for 20,000 dead Swedes"
reads one headline on their website!  The only ones who have any
understanding of the Bible, according to them, are those that agree with
them.

One of their fliers states that the nation's president "demonstrated his own
slavery to corruption" by  worshipping after his inauguration at St. John's
Episcopal Church (on 1/21) and at the National Cathedral (Episcopal).  The
flier refers to each church as a "sodomite whorehouse".

These views don't exactly line up with those, say, of the United Church of
Christ (which traces its heritage to the Pilgrims).

Do you have any doubt that the members of Westboro Baptist Church
(www.godhatesfags.com) aren't in the "quite there now" category, given the
sort of language from their website?

I *know* people like this.  I live in a neighborhood in which there are
*lots* of them.  There is only one right way, and that's their way.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> LX-i retorted:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "patriot" website.

The particular translation is actually irrelevant to the devout. It really 
doesn't matter what the Bible says; what is important is what it means. 
Often God didn't mean what He said and sometimes didn't say what He meant. 
So, like the Constitution, it comes down to interpretation.

In the Jewish tradition, the mechanism and the authority for interpretation 
was passed down with the text.

In the Catholic tradition, the authority for interpretation lies with the 
Church as an institution.

In the non-Catholic tradition, authority for interpretation lies with the 
individual invested with and assisted by the Holy Spirit. In this latter 
case the Bible verse "See Spot Run," could be God's absolute word on sex 
with plants.
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> LX-i retorted:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> the King James.

Yes, but further research might have shown that patriot.net is merely an 
ISP, much like my knology.net.  (More info is at 
http://www.patriot.net/aboutus.htm .)  I don't think that, if a church 
hosts their website there, that the association between religion and 
"knowledge" is disturbing...  ;)

> I have to agree that the *implication* that the King James Bible (of any
> vintage!) is THE patriotic Bible (implicit in its being so prominently
> featured in a website that also prominently features the American flag)

This is the same type of thing Freud did, but with sexuality instead. 
The Washington Monument has *got* to be sexual.  The fact that people 
always want to be #1, index finger raised, is a picture of a phallus, 
and number 2, created by the index and middle finger, is a picture of a 
vagina.  The numbers are sexist!

> I have acquaintances that make a big point of Demonstrating their Patriotism
> with a combination of American flags flying from their car's side windows
> with really-insulting anti-Islam stickers on their bumpers.

Free speech is a wonderful thing, no?  Just as I am annoyed at the car 
that park, on a military installation no less, with the W-and-a-slash 
sticker and the "when in doubt, start a war" sticker, I don't think they 
shouldn't be allowed to have them.

> I have run into the attitude that neither a Jew nor a Mormon can have
> anything positive to offer society in business, in society or in government
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lacey, as "mildly alarming".

People may have a preference for the King James Version of the Bible, 
and be vehement America supporters at the same time.  Must the two be 
related, simply because they occur on the same site?  I think you're 
making a medium-sized hill out of a molehill.  :)

> I had a co-worker inform me one time that his religion obligated him to haul
> me out in front of the workplace and, along with all the other True
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> management was not deluded into thinking that they did.

Misguided people are everywhere.  I glad you didn't get stoned on that 
particular occasion...

> Perhaps you have not personally encountered such circumstances.  But I have,
> and I have to agree with Peter's characterization of the association of
> "King James Version" with "patriot" as "mildly alarming".

This is like the Democrats being upset because when the word "Democrats" 
flew in on a Republican commercial, one frame had the last 4 letters. 
Do you remember that?  Unintentional, yes; but to hear the Democrats 
talk, you'd think that this was the subliminal proof they needed that 
Republicans were mean-spirited.

> I mistrust individuals as well as groups that identify themselves or their
> views with self-promoting adjectives like "patriotic", "humble", "expert",
> "proper Christian" and so forth.

Have you been around long enough, and in the right circles, to recognize 
the name Steve Winter?  Now *there* was someone who had it, shall we 
say, wrong...
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T03:46:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4aVPd.2085380$f47.365399@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com>`

```
I have created a COBOL program that does what this is asking for.  However, 
before looking at what I have done:

1) See separate note on the fact that my results match the "unique" word count - 
but not the "total" word counts - expected. (Don't ask me why).

2) My sample includes TONS of "debugging lines" that I kept in to show (COBOL 
readers) how I worked my way to this solution (sort of <G>)

3) Rather than using ANY "sort" statement, I have used a (temporary) INDEXED 
file - using "FS=02" to count duplicate words.  I can NOT believe this is the 
most "efficient" run-time solution, but it does show one "facility" of COBOL.

4) I compiled with Fujitsu - but used the @OPTIONS statement to check for 
non-Standard code.  Fujitsu is NOTORIOUSLY week and extension flagging, so I may 
have missed some extensions to the '85 Standard - but the only one I know about 
is using LINE SEQUENTIAL for ht input file.  (I even used A-/B-margin rules <G>)

  ***

I fully expect that are LOTS of other ways to do this in COBOL - certainly 
PERFORM loops may well out-perform UNSTRING and INSPECT statements (that I use 
*heavily*)

For what I have done, check out:

   http://home.comcast.net/~wmklein/DOX/WORDCNT.txt
```

```
It sounds like fun.  But it is already a solved problem in any language.

Use a 'text-tree' to order the words with an integer at each terminus to 
count unique words.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-15T09:53:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108490025.639649.93120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C004E9.00405915022005@knology.usenetserver.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
> Also, I am not sure what you mean by "terminus" -- to me this is
> the train station, or a term in its original latin form :-))

A 'terminal' or 'terminus' is not just any train or bus station, it is
the one at the end of the line or route. It is where the route
terminates.  In the case of a 'text-tree' it is where the branch that
represents one word terminates.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T23:08:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C004E9.00405915022005@knology.usenetserver.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
In article <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
 l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> .    On  15.02.05
>   wrote  joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I use the chars of the word as an index into a tree.  The terminus would 
be the node at the last character, there I would keep a counter for the 
number of instances.

You would create a node-item that looks something like:

   01 A-Node.
      05 occurs 27.
         10 an-ptr   usage pointer.
         10 an-cnt   pic s9(8) binary.
   

Then you would have a program like so:

   program-id. add2tree is recursive.
  * 
  * This program takes a word of length word-ll
  * and uses it as an index into the tree based
  * in anode.
  *
  * If a path exists, the count at the last indexed
  * node is incremented.
  * 
  * If the path does not exist, the tree nodes are 
  * allocated until all of the indexes are gone.
  *
  * E.G A tree with 'apple' and 'apply' and 'ape' 
  * would look thus:
  *
  * an['a']->an['p']--->an['p']->an['l']--->an['e'] = 1
  *                  |                   |
  *                  |->an['e'] = 1      |->an['y'] = 1
  *
  * As this is an example program, it is assumed that 
  * word is a-z or apos only.  No edits are done.
  *
   data division.
   working storage section.
   01 II          pic s9(8) binary.
   01 ch2ix       pix x(27) 
      value "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'".

   linkage section.
      copy anode.
   01 word-ll     pic s9(4) binary.
   01 word-txt    pic x(25).

   procedure division using anode
                            word-ll
                            word-txt.

      perform char-to-index
      if word-ll = 1
        perform increment-node
      else
        perform locate-node
        subtract 1 from word-ll
        call 'add2tree' using
           a-node
           word-ll
           word-txt (2:)
        end-call
      end-if

      goback.

  *
  * returns index of char in II
  *
   char-to-index.
      perform varying II from 1 by 1
        until (II > length of ch2ix)
           or (ch2ix (II) = word-txt (1:1))
        continue
      end-perform
      .      

  *
  * positions current node based on word(1), alloc if not found
  *
   locate-node.
      if an-ptr (II) = null
         ... do platform specific allocate of a 216-bytes here...
      end-if

      set address of A-Node
        to address of an-ptr (II)
      .      

  *
  * adds one to the terminal node in the tree for this word
  *
   increment-node.            
      add 1 to an-ctr (II)
      .      


Yes, yes, I know I could have removed the tail recursion if I really 
wanted to.  But it is cleaner this way, and with most words being in the 
sub 10-char range, it really doesn't make much difference.

Once all the words are passed to the program, just read through in 
pre-order and write out the count that represents the number of word 
instances.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T10:17:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  16.02.05
  wrote  joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


>> JZ> Use a 'text-tree' to order the words with an integer at each
>> JZ> terminus to count unique words.
>>
>>    How would that look in COBOL?


JZ> You would create a node-item that looks something like:
JZ>
JZ>    01 A-Node.
JZ>       05 occurs 27.
JZ>          10 an-ptr   usage pointer.
JZ>          10 an-cnt   pic s9(8) binary.
JZ>
JZ>
JZ> Then you would have a program like so:
JZ>
JZ>    program-id. add2tree is recursive.


   Ah, you see. It needs a recursive data structure and a recursive  
algorithm.

   But which COBOL compiler does support recursive calls of a program  
to itself?

   Is that regulated in some standard?

   BTW, I have written my version in COBOL, and runs only a little bit  
more than a minute (1:06,07) on a 350-MHz Windows NT 4.0 server with  
256 MByte RAM. The lookup for unique words is done by writing each and  
every word to an ISAM file which has as only field the word, which is  
the primary key. When the file status indicates "Duplicate key", I  
increment the count of unique words (the specification of that  
challenge does not require to count the frequency of the individual  
unique words). I rely completely on the Fujitsu run-time system to  
build an efficient tree for the index and to manage the file buffers.

   But :-(( the parser which I have presented in another message does  
not work correctly. For a lot of words it cuts one or more characters  
at the beginning of the word, and the counts are:

   my result  challenge
    16'747      12'691     unique words
   535'208     789'781     total words


   So it still needs some work.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

A. Der Mann hat viele Kinder. B. Ja, aber ich glaube, von den meisten hat er bloï¿½ die Korrektur besorgt.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T15:22:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2F2Rd.2501972$f47.445411@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com> <9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
IBM (mainframe  & PC) and most other workstation compilers support recursion 
(and have for several years)

Check your compiler for "local-storage" section.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T08:48:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cv2htc$2ock$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com> <9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...

>    Ah, you see. It needs a recursive data structure and a recursive
> algorithm.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Is that regulated in some standard?

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 allows recursive programs, methods and functions.  See the
description of and rules for the RECURSIVE clause in 11.9, PROGRAM-ID
paragraph on Pages 186-187.

Recursive calls were explicitly disallowed by ANSI X3.23-1985 / ISO
1989-1985 (page X-30, 5.2, The CALL statement, general rule 15) as well as
ANSI X3.23-1974.  .

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T09:40:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108662054.545867.263710@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <9QwEwCfeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-20564A.23080316022005@knology.usenetserver.com> <9R3Z1DEeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
> Ah, you see. It needs a recursive data structure and a recursive
algorithm.

There is no requirement for the compiler to support recursion, it can
be done equally well with iteration.

In fact all that recursion does is push a new stack frame.  It is easy
enough to maintain your own 'stack' using an array and 'recurse' in a
perform loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T15:00:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<111scngmtq9s446@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <111q4nm73lgvk8c@corp.supernews.com> <e2927$421e148b$45491f85$5170@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:e2927$421e148b$45491f85$5170@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to learn...

This is the subprogram that implemented the binary tree. This is not
the same solution used for the ANSI 85 conforming program.

      $set optspeed noalter notrickle noparamcountcheck
       identification division.
       program-id. bin-tree.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 heap-id pic x(2) comp-5.
       01 status-word pic x(2).
       01 deepest-probe pic x(2) comp-5 value 0.
       01 probe-depth pic x(2) comp-5.
       01 number-of-probes pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
       01 sum-of-levels pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
       01 stats-display.
         02 pic x value space.
         02 deepest-probe-display pic zzz9.
         02 pic x value space.
         02 average-probe-display pic zzz9.9.
         02 pic x(6) value space.
       01 root-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 new-node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
       01 node-size pic x(4) comp-5 value 32.
       01 flags pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
       01 status-code pic x(2) comp-5.
       01 node.
         02 l-node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
         02 key-field pic x(18).
         02  pic x(2).
         02 r-node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
         02  pic x(4).
       01 new-node.
         02 new-l-node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
         02 new-key-field pic x(18).
         02  pic x(2).
         02 new-r-node-ptr pic x(4) comp-5.
         02  pic x(4).
       linkage section.
       01 insert-status pic x.
         88 init value "i".
         88 clean-up value "c".
         88 failure value "0".
         88 success value "1".
       01 word-text pic x(18).
       procedure division using insert-status word-text.
       begin section.
           if clean-up
               call "CBL_CLOSE_VFILE" using by value heap-id
                 returning status-code
               move deepest-probe to deepest-probe-display
               compute average-probe-display =
                   sum-of-levels / number-of-probes
               move stats-display to word-text
               exit program
           end-if
           if init
               move 0 to root-ptr
               move 0 to node-ptr
               call "CBL_OPEN_VFILE" using heap-id status-word
                 returning status-code
           end-if
           if root-ptr = 0
               perform init-new-node
               move new-node-ptr to root-ptr
               set success to true
               exit program
           end-if
           move root-ptr to node-ptr
           move 0 to probe-depth
           add 1 to number-of-probes
           perform until exit
               add 1 to probe-depth
               perform read-node
               if key-field = word-text
                   set failure to true
                   exit perform
               end-if
               if word-text < key-field
                   if l-node-ptr = 0
                       perform init-new-node
                       move new-node-ptr to l-node-ptr
                       perform write-node
                       set success to true
                       exit perform
                   end-if
                   move l-node-ptr to node-ptr
                   exit perform cycle
               end-if
               if word-text > key-field
                   if r-node-ptr = 0
                       perform init-new-node
                       move new-node-ptr to r-node-ptr
                       perform write-node
                       set success to true
                       exit perform
                   end-if
                   move r-node-ptr to node-ptr
                   exit perform cycle
               end-if
           end-perform
           add probe-depth to sum-of-levels
           if probe-depth > deepest-probe
               move probe-depth to deepest-probe
           end-if
           exit program
           .
       init-new-node section.
           add node-size to new-node-ptr
           move spaces to new-node
           move 0 to new-l-node-ptr
           move 0 to new-r-node-ptr
           move word-text to new-key-field
           call "CBL_WRITE_VFILE" using by value heap-id
               new-node-ptr node-size by reference new-node
             returning status-code
           if status-code not = 0
               set failure to true
               exit program
           end-if
           .
       read-node section.
           call "CBL_READ_VFILE" using by value heap-id
               node-ptr node-size by reference node
             returning status-code
           if status-code not = 0
               set failure to true
               exit program
           end-if
           .
       write-node section.
           call "CBL_WRITE_VFILE" using by value heap-id
               node-ptr node-size by reference node
             returning status-code
           if status-code not = 0
               set failure to true
               exit program
           end-if
           .
       end program bin-tree.
```

```
More "specification" clarification needed.

1) Am I correct that you are ignoring (in your "words" the "00n : 00n" in 
columns 1-7 (i.e. chapter and verse information)

2) Do you want to count "1" in "1 Samuel" and "2" in "2 Samuel" (etc) as words?

3) Are you treating hyphenated words as a single word, e.g.
  "BRICK-KILN" as one or two words in the line

"IRON  AND MADE THEM PASS THROUGH THE BRICK-KILN  AND THUS DID  "
```

```
I have been able to reproduce SOME of the expected results of:

> total words = 789781
> unique words = 12691

words as TWO separate words),

    +789715

input words.  I wonder what the original poster was counting that I am not?

input words BEFORE eliminating numbers?)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Word-processing challenge anyone?

- **From:** epc8@juno.com

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> My COBOL program does get 12691 for "unique" words (when I treat
hyphenated
> words as TWO separate words), However, I am only getting +789715
input words.

I have not seen your source code yet due to Google latency, but I
suspect that you are not counting the word 'book' that appears in the
first 8 columns. It appears 66 times. That added to your total gives
the correct number of total words.
```

```
Thanks for the correction.  You are right.

I asked (earlier) about what to do with columns 1-8 - and I decided just to 
ignore them (before seeing the rule about converting numbers to spaces).

My code is now posted (and could EASILY be changed if I really wanted to pick up 
those "BOOK" in columns 1-8)

For those NOT in comp.lang.cobol, the source code (with LOTS of extraneous 
debugging information) is at:
   http://home.comcast.net/~wmklein/DOX/WORDCNT.txt

However, if you aren't reading this in comp.lang.cobol, please note that I 
commented there on lots of "performance tuning" things that could be done - if 
that was the goal.  (Also it would be prettier - if using some of the extensions 
available in many PC and Unix COBOL compilers)

For the person asking about "squeezing out" apostrophes, this is done with 
INSPECT TALLYING (to figure out where they are) and then REFERENCE MODIFICATION 
to move the data - to get rid of them.
```

```
Corrected COBOL code (to handle "BOOK" in columns 1-8) is now posted and 
available (to anyone with a COBOL compiler <G> ... Sorry about that PL/I and 
Fortran people) at:

  http://home.comcast.net/~wmklein/DOX/WORDCNT.txt

For those who don't like "verbose" COBOL, there are LOTS of ways this could be 
cut down.  I coded this for "understandability" (for the average COBOL 
programmer) - not for "terseness" - which could be done *IF* I saw that as a 
desirable goal.

As stated in another post, this is "fully conforming" ANSI/ISO 1985 (not even 
2002) source code with the single exception that it expects "line sequential" 
input file.  (Removing the single word "line" from the Select/Assign clause) 
would make this valid source code for any "mainframe" or other environment using 
"record sequential" text files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Word-processing challenge anyone?

- **Message-ID:** `<6eGdnYleAJG4zY3fRVn-uQ@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108354693.482591.70020@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<420f4915$0$38863$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <XFQPd.1786116$B07.269262@news.easynews.com> <KRRPd.1791168$B07.270156@news.easynews.com> <hZUPd.1802836$B07.271252@news.easynews.com> <1108352420.785673.316960@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <5dVPd.1804097$B07.271597@news.easynews.com> <1108354693.482591.70020@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>>there on lots of "performance tuning" things that could be done - if
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> better.

> Unless you can blow them away with a 3 line program that runs 10 times
> faster and could be understood by a 5 year old then there is probably
> no point in feeding their ego.

Here is how I do it in AWK, including removing the text before
(but not including) the Genesis line.  My first version also removed
that one, as I thought that was requested, and also the lines
at the end.   It makes sense for programs to do all the work, instead
of only part of it.

The actual unique word identification is done by the statement

words[tolower($i)]++;

and counting unique words by the statement

for(i in words) w++;

Three statements to not count the beginning and end.

Until PL/I, Fortran or COBOL get associative arrays, it will take
a lot more code to do problems like this.  It isn't hard to do in Java
as there is a Hashtable class in the standard class libraries.

A few years ago I did one like this in Java, including printing out the 
high frequency words on about 18GB of text.  It ran for about a day, 
which isn't much longer than it takes to read in the whole file.



BEGIN {start=systime();}
/Genesis/ { flag=1;}
/^End/ { flag=0;}
{
    if(!flag) next;
    gsub("'","");
    gsub("[^A-Za-z]"," ");
    t += NF;
    for(i=1;i<=NF;i++) words[tolower($i)]++;
}
END {
    for(i in words) w++;
    print "total words",t,"unique words",w,"in",systime()-start,"Sec";
}

total words 789781 unique words 12691 in 18 Sec on a 350MHz P-II.

-- glen
```

## Thread: moving data from one place to another in a text file — 101 this month / 211 total

### moving data from one place to another in a text file

- **From:** "francesco" <fontana.bleu@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T04:02:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
I have a COBOL file that reads an entire text file and gives as an
output the same input file with another name. The problem is that in
the input file the last two lines (that contain numerical values only)
must be located some place else in the output file. How can I achieve
this ?

Thank you

Francesco
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T13:15:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Qs7Nf6uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
.    On  14.02.05
  wrote  fontana.bleu@libero.it (francesco)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com
  about  moving data from one place to another in a text file


fb> I have a COBOL file that reads an entire text file and gives as an
fb> output the same input file with another name. The problem is that in
fb> the input file the last two lines (that contain numerical values
fb> only) must be located some place else in the output file. How can I
fb> achieve this ?

   Is it know beforehand, where in the file these last two lines have  
to be inserted? Both in one location or at different ones?

   If yes, just split the file at those places where these two lines  
have to be inserted. Write all records to the output file, until you  
have reached the place where the newly inserted have to go, divert the  
rest of the file into a temporary file, then write those two  
insertables, and copy the temporary file to the actual outpï¿½ut file.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Gerade das Gegenteil tun heiï¿½t auch nachahmen, es heiï¿½t nï¿½mlich das Gegenteil nachahmen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-14T10:22:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108405357.508058.199130@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
> the last two lines (that contain numerical values only)
> must be located some place else in the output file.

Read through the file and store each line in two buffers in
working-storage such that when the 'at end' is reached these are the
last two lines.

ie

|    perform until endoffile
|        read infile at end set endoffile
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
|    end-perform

then restart the file reading and writing inserting the two records
where required and dropping them when read.
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T11:20:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 14 Feb 2005 04:02:41 -0800, "francesco" <fontana.bleu@libero.it>
wrote:

>I have a COBOL file that reads an entire text file and gives as an
>output the same input file with another name. The problem is that in
>the input file the last two lines (that contain numerical values only)
>must be located some place else in the output file. How can I achieve
>this ?

1. Read the file into a linked list. 
2. Re-link the last two lines into their new location. 
3. Write the list to an output file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T02:21:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <9Q-RodD9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 17 Feb 2005 19:47:41 -0500, Binyamin Dissen
<postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>On 16 Feb 2005 17:02:00 GMT l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The "linkage" need not be addresses.

Correct, it can be done with subscripts .. at the cost of wasting
memory by padding each line with spaces and limiting the maximum
number of lines to some arbitrary number. 

Why not do it right? Should we forever be limited by a mediocre
programmer who left in the '70s .. or hung around and is now about to
reture?

linkage section.
01  line-entry.
     05  next-line     pointer.
     05  text-length binary pic s9(04).
     05  line-text.
           10   occurs 1 to 1000 depending on text-length pic x.

procedure division.

    open input-file
    perform read-input
    perform until end-of-file
     compute alloc-length = input-length
           + length of next-line + length of text-length
     call 'malloc' using by value alloc-length 
                      returning  next-line
*> per the 2002 Standard the above becomes
*> allocate alloc-length returning next-line
      set address of line-entry to next-line
      set next-line to null
      move input-length to text-length
      move input-text to line-text
      perform read-input
    end-perform
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T19:44:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <9Q-RodD9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com>`

```
> Should we forever be limited by a mediocre programmer who left in the
'70s ..

No, we should follow your example and do it like C programmers did in
the 80s, before they discovered modern programming.

Your code may well show how clever you think you are by changing the
problem into a different problem, but it does not actually solve what
was required.  The data started as a serial set of lines in a file and
is now a serial set of lines in a list.

An actual solution may be:

       open infile
       until eof:
           read infile
                 at end set eof
                 not at end
                       move b to a
                       move inrec to b
           end-read
       close infile

       unset eof
       open infile
       open outfile
       until eof:
            read infile
                  at end set eof
                  not at end
                  if inrec = a or b )
                       continue
                  else
                      if ( this is where to insert )
                           write outrec from a
                           write outrec from b
                      end-if
                      write outrec from inrec
                  end-if
              end-read
         close infile
         close outfile

Why complicate it with irrelevant stuff.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T04:38:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <9Q-RodD9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com> <1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 17 Feb 2005 19:44:41 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Should we forever be limited by a mediocre programmer who left in the
>'70s ..
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>An actual solution may be:
[snip]
>Why complicate it with irrelevant stuff.

The general solution to this class of problem is called a Text Editor.
On Unix, it could be scripted with SED or AWK. Since the request was
for a Cobol solution, I provided a generalized one: convert the file
to a list and manipulate the lines therein. That's what text editors
do. It's cleaner than your solution of reading the file twice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T21:08:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

- **References:** `<1108382561.342297.3430@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <9Q-RodD9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com> <1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com>`

```
> Since the request was for a Cobol solution, I provided a generalized
one:
> convert the file to a list and manipulate the lines therein.

In what way is it 'general' if it requires a specific version of the
compiler and vendor extensions in some of the currently available
compilers.

> It's cleaner than your solution of reading the file twice.

Whereas your 'solution' reads the file once and then needs to 'read'
the list twice or more, which you haven't even coded yet.  Why is 3
passes over the data with a media change 'cleaner' than 2 ?

You, once again, want to confuse Cobol students with 'what C did 20
years ago'.
```

```
>> Since the request was for a Cobol solution, I provided a generalized
>one:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers.

It doesn't require a specific compiler version. Malloc is a C function
that's been unchanged since the '70s.

>> It's cleaner than your solution of reading the file twice.
>
>Whereas your 'solution' reads the file once and then needs to 'read'
>the list twice or more, which you haven't even coded yet.  Why is 3
>passes over the data with a media change 'cleaner' than 2 ?

Because a file system's pass over the data is many times slower than
doing it with direct addressing. 

>You, once again, want to confuse Cobol students with 'what C did 20
>years ago'.

I want to educate them in programming fundamentals.
```

```
> It doesn't require a specific compiler version. Malloc is a C
function
> that's been unchanged since the '70s.

USAGE POINTER is a MF extension in current '85 compilers.

Not all machines have a C library available for linking into the
compiled program.

> Because a file system's pass over the data is many times slower than
> doing it with direct addressing.

Since when did 'cleaner' mean 'faster' ?

> >You, once again, want to confuse Cobol students with 'what C did 20
> >years ago'.

> I want to educate them in programming fundamentals.

Fiddling with machine address may be fundemental, but it assembly
level.  The guy is trying to learn Cobol, not bit fiddling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ USAGE POINTER (was: moving data from one place to another in a text f)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-02-20T09:41:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9RFxiN09flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <ghge115jgcsj9jqcrdodletvo4krmildlp@4ax.com> <1108877884.885222.23270@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
.    On  19.02.05
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  1108877884.885222.23270@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com
  about  Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file


>> Pointer syntax is the same on MF, IBM and Fujitsu.

   I have no clues if that is true, but regarding this one:

r> As I may have mentioned before: Fujitsu don't have that usage
r> documented, it isn't in their syntax definition, it isn't supported.

   May I quote from the "Language Reference Manual" for Fujitsu COBOL  
3.0, the free student edition, which one can be download (the  
documentation independently from the compiler)
   from http://www.adtools.com/student/index.htm#3

--------- schnipp -----------------------------------------

106 Chapter 2. COBOL Modules

Pointer Handling

Use pointers for referencing the address area.

The address of an area can be found using the ADDR function
and set in the pointer data item. A pointer data item defines the
data description entry written in the USAGE IS POINTER clause.

You define the structure attributes of the area to be referenced
using a pointer in the BASED-STORAGE SECTION of the DATA
DIVISION. The data item area defined in the BASED-STORAGE
SECTION cannot be reserved.

Clauses can be described both in the data description entry of the
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION and also the BASED ON clause
written in the data description entry of the BASED-STORAGE
SECTION. Write the BASED ON clause to specify an implicit
pointer.

When referencing a data item defined in the BASED-STORAGE
SECTION, a pointer qualifier or an implicit pointer must be
added to the data-name.

------------------ schnapp --------------------------------


>> That covers more than 90% of compilers in use.

r> Please provide the data that shows this to be the case.  Or did you
r> just pull these figures out your arse and you seem to prefer to do.

   Fujitsu is 100% of the COBOL compilers which I do use...


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Diese ganze Lehre taugt zu nichts als darï¿½ber zu disputieren. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
On 19 Feb 2005 21:38:04 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> Pointer syntax is the same on MF, IBM and Fujitsu.
>
>As I may have mentioned before: Fujitsu don't have that usage
>documented, it isn't in their syntax definition, it isn't supported.

Don't confuse 'endorsed' with 'suported'. Based on what I've read,
Fujitsu Support is an oxymoron.

Interesting to note that the 2002 Standard does reluctantly endorse
the old syntax:

SET ADDRESS OF dataname TO pointer

It requires, however, that dataname be 'based'. Thus, the above is
effectively: 

SET basedpointer TO pointer

Still, the retro syntax will help conversion of old 'industry
standard' programs. (If we ever get around to converting them.)

>> That covers more than 90% of compilers in use.
>
>Please provide the data that shows this to be the case.  Or did you
>just pull these figures out your arse and you seem to prefer to do.

In the absence of publically available marketing data, I estimated
based on experience.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T12:45:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvg5l8$2lg5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com> <1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <1108790180.933902.121970@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <ghge115jgcsj9jqcrdodletvo4krmildlp@4ax.com> <1108877884.885222.23270@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <uvqg11h83555sombto7rhl9evba2kqf9gh@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:uvqg11h83555sombto7rhl9evba2kqf9gh@4ax.com...

> Interesting to note that the 2002 Standard does reluctantly endorse
> the old syntax:

I'm curious as to how you have come to the conclusion that a book has
behaved reluctantly.

I am also curious as to why a feature *new* to ISO/IEC 1989:2002 COBOL and
absent from any prior COBOL standard is factually "old syntax" when the
alternative seems to be, at best, "a vendor-specific extension to ANSI
X3.23-1985 COBOL syntax that is incompatible with the 2002 and subsequent
COBOL standards".

What did the authors of these particular portions of the 2002 standard have
to say when you brought your alternative syntax and funtionality up to them
in your correspondence during the drafting and approval processes for that
standard?

> In the absence of publically available marketing data, I estimated
> based on experience.

The source of your estimates appears to be similar to that from which you
obtained your certain knowledge of the motivation of the behavior of books,
as well as your knowledge of the relative age of syntactic elements in
standard COBOL that are new as compared with those that were rejected in the
course of development of the standard.    Could you provide some location
more referenceable than memories extracted from your own head (or from other
portions of your anatomy)?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 22 Feb 2005 12:45:27 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL standards".

The old syntax has been an industry standard (an oxymoron?) for many
years. It wasn't incompatible with the 2002 Standard because it
predated the Standard. On the contrary, the Standard chose to be
incompatible with prior art.

>What did the authors of these particular portions of the 2002 standard have
>to say when you brought your alternative syntax and funtionality up to them
>in your correspondence during the drafting and approval processes for that
>standard?

They were familiar with de facto standards. I had no reason to write
because I had nothing to add.

>> In the absence of publically available marketing data, I estimated
>> based on experience.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>portions of your anatomy)?

Sure. Search job ads on sites like DICE and Monster. The demand for
experience with IBM mainframe and Micro Focus Cobol vs. others is a
fairly accurate measure of compiler/platform usage in the REAL WORLD. 

You'll find I was being conservative when I said 90%. It's well over
95%.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-23T08:36:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vqa6115uro7gku7ggl32oq08an1mrp8frp@4ax.com> <9Q-RodD9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com> <1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com>`

```
My honest opinion is that "operating environments" are almost always better
at managing memory and other system resources than the end-user programmer .
I am less than enamored with ALLOCATE / FREE and would suggest that they be
used sparingly.  That's actually a side point.

Your position seems to have been that this is a *general* solution
applicable *today* using *existing* compilers.  Which *available* compilers
are compliant with ISO/IEC 1989:2002?

I denigrate a feature of ISO/IEC 1989:2002 for *current* compilers and
*current* environments because the overwhelming majority of them are
compliant with ANSI X3.23-1985, and it is not yet clear to me that all
implementations conforming to that standard approach the subject of dynamic
memory using either the same semantics or the same syntax, *if at all*.   A
"general solution" requires *general availability*, not merely *availability
in the environments I find interesting*.  And "general availability" results
from "customer demand".  Don't blame the *vendors* for failing to provide a
feature of the current COBOL standard in a compiler compliant with a prior
one if the *customers* don't ask for it.  The same is true of *vendors*
electing not to produce a compiler fully-compliant with all specifications
of ISO/IEC 1989:2002; it's an expensive proposition, and investing large
sums of money in a new product with little hope of recouping that investment
through sales of that product is not generally considered good business
practice.

Why do I denigrate a solution provided in ISO/IEC 1989:2002?  Because that
solution is not available as a standard feature in most standards-compliant
COBOL implementations today, given that most of them conform to ANSI
X3.23-1985 (and some are still marketed that conform to ANSI X3.23-1974).  A
solution that does not provide exactly the same results with exactly the
same syntax in all implementations is not a "general" solution.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com...
> Why do you denegrate a solution supported by the 2002 Cobol Standard?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >doesn't provide his "general solution" is of no interest to any
programmer
> >with any talent or intelligence  ... ;-(
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> "malloc"; HOWEVER,  I also know of some CURRENTLY USED systems where
this
> >> wouldn't get past the compile stage.)
> >>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >>> Since the request was for a Cobol solution, I provided a
generalized
> >> >>one:
> >> >>> convert the file to a list and manipulate the lines therein.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> > It doesn't require a specific compiler version. Malloc is a C
function
> >> > that's been unchanged since the '70s.
> >> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
On Wed, 23 Feb 2005 08:36:31 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Why do I denigrate a solution provided in ISO/IEC 1989:2002?  Because that
>solution is not available as a standard feature in most standards-compliant
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>same syntax in all implementations is not a "general" solution.

What are we practitioners supposed to do while the Standards process
takes its liesurely pace? We can't afford to take a five-year hiatus.
My solution is to use syntax that works the same on IBM, Micro Focus
and Fujitsu. I avoid the ALLOCATE verb, available on Micro Focus and
the '02 Standard, because it isn't available on the other two. In its
place I use C's malloc(), which is available on most serious
development machines. 

If you have a PRACTICAL solution that works on more than the three
best-selling compilers, I'm listening.
```

```
The original text of this thread's message was,

"I have a COBOL file that reads an entire text file and gives as an
output the same input file with another name. The problem is that in
the input file the last two lines (that contain numerical values only)
must be located some place else in the output file. How can I achieve
this ?

Thank you

Francesco"

Clearly, that can't be what you are claiming cannot be done in a Standard ('85, 
'02 - or even '68 or '74) manner.  As people have changed the "topic" so much 
without changing the subject, can you tell me what you are asking for a (truly) 
generalized solution for?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T09:11:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375r11l7lpdk0v9rl93l1sv2pmnv3pithf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <NHbTd.3222897$f47.578404@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Thu, 24 Feb 2005 03:18:05 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
>news:2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>generalized solution for?

I was referring to the general problem of lists. Given that two lines
need to be moved, they can be stored in a two entry array and the
input file read twice. A general version of the problem would call for
moving m lines, where m can be zero thru the file size (n). An example
is sorting the file. Sorting could be done with a two entry array, but
the result would, on average, require copying n^2 records (assuming
one input and output file per pass).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T14:05:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7blTd.2975163$B07.471957@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <NHbTd.3222897$f47.578404@news.easynews.com> <375r11l7lpdk0v9rl93l1sv2pmnv3pithf@4ax.com>`

```
Personally, I would say that the "read twice" solution is the ONLY truly 
practical solution - as any solution requiring "memory allocation" canNOT handle 
potentially "available memory-size + 1" sized file input - while the read twice 
method can.

And, of course, the "read twice method" would be portable to EVERY ANSI/ISO 
conforming compiler from the '68 Standard on.

As with the cross-language challenge, if a "real world" solution were required 
to be the "fastest" possible *AND* the size of the input file were smaller than 
the "probable" available memory, then an "in memory solution" could be created 
(but that could also use an ANSI/ISO Standard table - or an ANSI/ISO Standard 
SORT - or any of a number of "practical" and PORTABLE solutions)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T10:24:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109269490.351368.158610@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<375r11l7lpdk0v9rl93l1sv2pmnv3pithf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <NHbTd.3222897$f47.578404@news.easynews.com> <375r11l7lpdk0v9rl93l1sv2pmnv3pithf@4ax.com>`

```
> I was referring to the general problem of lists.

Lists aren't a 'general problem'.

> A general version of the problem would call for
> moving m lines, where m can be zero thru the file size (n)

'Zero' and 'file size' means no lines moved.  In fact the maximum of
this specific type of problem is 'half the file'.

> An example is sorting the file.

The general solution to 'sorting the file' is sorting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-25T02:04:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kns11hej65j93o4p3kk1g71f71li5ebe9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <NHbTd.3222897$f47.578404@news.easynews.com> <375r11l7lpdk0v9rl93l1sv2pmnv3pithf@4ax.com> <1109269490.351368.158610@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 24 Feb 2005 10:24:50 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> A general version of the problem would call for
>> moving m lines, where m can be zero thru the file size (n)
>
>'Zero' and 'file size' means no lines moved.  In fact the maximum of
>this specific type of problem is 'half the file'.

When output order is the reverse of input order, the number of lines
moved is n - 1. When records are shuffled like a deck of cards, the
number moved is n (unless you're planning to 'deal bottoms').

>> An example is sorting the file.
>
>The general solution to 'sorting the file' is sorting.

Sorting is a special case of Reordering Objects. The fastest way to
reorder objects in an ordered collection is to exchange pointers, as
opposed to physically moving the objects.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T11:02:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<q3d9111hf8n3l7tuel1irc31l1i0slb5mf@4ax.com> <6via11hg4sd1k5c3mv33pn8tmusgfdga2l@4ax.com> <1108698281.178522.63150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com...

> What are we practitioners supposed to do while the Standards process
> takes its liesurely pace? We can't afford to take a five-year hiatus.

What five-year hiatus?  For the features you've specified, the *standards*
process is done.

> My solution is to use syntax that works the same on IBM, Micro Focus
> and Fujitsu.

Goody.  It is not a *general* solution if it makes use of
*implementor-defined* extensions.

> I avoid the ALLOCATE verb, available on Micro Focus and
> the '02 Standard, because it isn't available on the other two.

If it isn't available on the other two, it's because they don't conform (or
claim to conform) to the '02 standard.  That is the fault of neither the
standards committee nor the implementation.

> In its place I use C's malloc(),

Available in which COBOL standard?  According to what *standard* calling
methodology?

> which is available on most serious development machines.

Define "serious".

> If you have a PRACTICAL solution that works on more than the three
> best-selling compilers, I'm listening.

Are you contending that there is *no* practical solution that can be
developed *without* making use of non-portable vendor-specific implementor
extensions to standard COBOL?

As to "best-selling compilers", our *best selling* COBOL compiler is
COBOL74, and to try to get our customer base to move to COBOL85 we made it
quite a bit more expensive.  Thus far it hasn't worked.

A *general* solution works in all COBOL environments.  If it doesn't work
in, or even isn't appropriate for, *all* COBOL environments, it fails the
test of being a *general* solution.

Or are you changing the qualifier from "general" to "PRACTICAL" on us?

     -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-27T11:12:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f1t321l54qf3vl7fguh8l9j3gef9o21u5h@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <jcps1157v8lrkd7rh1dg8k2bt761a939kg@4ax.com> <1109297999.928079.94980@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <pklv11to0ulse8is31lsqrgqbok8e5cicf@4ax.com> <cvpr4c$5ga$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 26 Feb 2005 07:47:08 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <pklv11to0ulse8is31lsqrgqbok8e5cicf@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>This seems to be a Brooklyn Bridge defense, Mr Wagner.

Brooklyn Bridge is Argumentum Ad Populum -- the greater the number of
people believing a proposition, the more likely it is to be true. I
didn't claim mainframers are in the majority. They're not.

>>A
>>month ago, their dogmatism and intolerance cost me $5K.
>
>So you find their actions to be so Good and Noble that you imitate them... 
>mainframers do (x) so you do it, as well?

On the contrary, my skepticism about (x) is increased.

>>My rants are not abstract; they're based on real life.
>
>They may be based on *a* life - yours - certainly enough... but one might 
>hope there's a bit more to the world than what is found in the small set 
>of accidents which define a given existence.

In logic, The Fallacy of Accident is committed when a general rule is
applied to a particular case whose "accidental" circumstances mean
that the rule is inapplicable.  It is the error made when one goes
from the general to the specific. 

You allege the opposite: The Fallacy of Converse Accident or Hasty
Generalization, wherein one forms a general rule by examining only a
few specific cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-02-28T09:17:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvv94t$4rh$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <pklv11to0ulse8is31lsqrgqbok8e5cicf@4ax.com> <cvpr4c$5ga$1@panix5.panix.com> <f1t321l54qf3vl7fguh8l9j3gef9o21u5h@4ax.com>`

```
In article <f1t321l54qf3vl7fguh8l9j3gef9o21u5h@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 26 Feb 2005 07:47:08 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>people believing a proposition, the more likely it is to be true.

Not quite, Mr Wagner.  The Brooklyn Bridge argument is an attempt to 
justify one's own behavior based on similar or identical behaviors of 
others... or *other*, and note the singular.  It applies equally well to:

'But everyone *else* is (doing (x))!'

'So everyone else jumps off the Brooklyn Bridge... you will, too?'

... and this applies almost to the Argumentum you cite.  However, consider 
the cases of

'But Jimmy, Janey, Billy and Barbara are (doing (x))!'...

... and ...

'But Kristen is (doing (x))!'

... and the response ending with '... jumps off the Brooklyn Bridge... you 
will, to?'

>I
>didn't claim mainframers are in the majority. They're not.

Nobody said you made such a claim, Mr Wagner, but in that you responded to 
an evaluation of your own behavior ('you insulted...') with an example of 
behavior by others ('I have been attacked') you appear to indulge in a 
Brooklyn Bridge defense.

>
>>>A
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>On the contrary, my skepticism about (x) is increased.

That makes your responding to evaluations of your own behavior with 
examples from their even more... curious.

'I do (y)... and if you ask about it I'll point out that the people I am 
skeptical of do (y), too!'

>
>>>My rants are not abstract; they're based on real life.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>few specific cases. 

I state, Mr Wagner, that what is based on '*a* life - yours -' (emphasis 
original) might be based on what is possibly a subset of what is to be 
found in the world... nothing more, nothing less.  If the small set of 
accidents which make up your single existence are the totality of that 
which is 'the world' then, it would seem, I am do well to re-evaluate my 
statement.

DD
```

- **Message-ID:** `<0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109272803.722372.291640@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 24 Feb 2005 11:20:03 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:


>RP>> Whereas your 'solution' reads the file once and then needs to
>'read'
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>non-solution.

A benchmark isn't required to determine thousands of instructions, in
the file system, take longer than several inline instructions. 

On the other hand, I've said many abuses during the Good Old Days
occurred because programmers were forced to hold the hand of
underpowered hardware. Now that we have CPU cycles to burn, we should
do right rather than sacrifice style to necessity. Put everything in a
database and don't worry about speed.

On the other other hand, I have weekly jobs that run 24 hours, during
which they max out 16 CPUs. That's excessive. All they're doing is
'database stuff' on millions of rows. We discover we can speed up
('optimize')  the jobs by partitioning the database and modifying the
APPLICATION to run parallel processes. If parm=low, process keys
beginning with 0-4; if parm=high, process keys beginning with 5-9.
Shades of the Good Old Days. Applications are back to holding the
platform's hand. The only difference is the platform has become the
database.

Plus CA Change, Plus C'Est La Meme Chose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-25T18:26:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109384777.990755.219630@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <1108703326.879934.273560@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109272803.722372.291640@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com>`

```
> A benchmark isn't required to determine thousands of instructions, in
> the file system, take longer than several inline instructions.

Do you believe that a pass over your list is 'several' instructions ?

In any case the 2nd pass will be from the cache.

> Now that we have CPU cycles to burn, we should
> do right rather than sacrifice style to necessity.

Now that we have CPU cycles to burn the emphasis should be on saving
programmer time.  There is, as you say, no longer any need to continue
doing 'what C did in the 70s'.

> Put everything in a database and don't worry about speed.

That's it.

> On the other other hand, I have weekly jobs that run 24 hours, during
> which they max out 16 CPUs.

How strange then that you claimed, just a couple of days ago:

>> Now, processor chips are so inexpensive and plentiful that they're
idle most of the time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-27T11:12:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01u321lt9hf16ef6f6utoae8d96v2nf1mt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109272803.722372.291640@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <1109384777.990755.219630@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 25 Feb 2005 18:26:18 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> A benchmark isn't required to determine thousands of instructions, in
>> the file system, take longer than several inline instructions.
>
>Do you believe that a pass over your list is 'several' instructions ?

Stepping to the next entry takes several instructions; reading the
next record from a file takes thousands of instructions.

>In any case the 2nd pass will be from the cache.

Data will be in a cache; instructions will not be.

>> Now that we have CPU cycles to burn, we should
>> do right rather than sacrifice style to necessity.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>doing 'what C did in the 70s'.

Agreed. Time is saved by creating tools such as the Python method you
demonstrated. What was sloppy programming under old paradigms is still
sloppy, it just runs faster.

>> On the other other hand, I have weekly jobs that run 24 hours, during
>> which they max out 16 CPUs.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>idle most of the time.

Both are true. CPUs are idle six days a week and maxed out on the
seventh.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-27T11:12:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tou321pokh158klm1deo3dp1bnreb653mo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vmad111l0h49nmhnh1vcb6mp0gjioki38e@4ax.com> <p2dSd.2905172$f47.522482@news.easynews.com> <cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109272803.722372.291640@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <ivl021hls0u0ltivghgmignkj66r5savih@4ax.com>`

```
On Sat, 26 Feb 2005 11:47:42 +0000, Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pentium performs about as well as "Supercalc" used to on an 8MHz 286?

That's a good example because I worked on a spreadsheet like, but
faster than, Supercalc. Its speed came from hand-crafted Intel
assembly language. Porting to another hardware platform (Casio Boss)
required a complete rewrite. Porting to another OS, Win95, required a
major rewrite of the user interface. As complexity increased, adding
features became more and more difficult. 

The bloat in products like Excel free it from the stagnation of
platform and shallow product architecture. It eliminates the hidden
linkages found in 'small' software by adding speed-absorbing layers of
abstraction. It speeds up Time To Market and extends the useful life
of the product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from one place to another in a text file

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>On Sat, 26 Feb 2005 11:47:42 +0000, Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
>wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>platform and shallow product architecture.

Pardon?  I was under the impression that it only ran on a Wintel
platform.
  
>.. It eliminates the hidden
>linkages found in 'small' software by adding speed-absorbing layers of
>abstraction.

I fail to see how any software specifically coded using the windows
API can be said to contain much in the way of abstraction.

>.. It speeds up Time To Market and extends the useful life
>of the product. 

"Useful life"??  It's revamped every ten minutes.  Rarely compatibly.
:-)
```

- **References:** `<cvg5r1$2lgf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3dpn119ol03uumjqlrj4tctk118pda6lpf@4ax.com> <cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109272803.722372.291640@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <ivl021hls0u0ltivghgmignkj66r5savih@4ax.com> <tou321pokh158klm1deo3dp1bnreb653mo@4ax.com> <h61421l5ve9743grogkih3nbj62elmme45@4ax.com>`

```
On Sun, 27 Feb 2005 17:39:23 +0000, Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>platform.

>>.. It eliminates the hidden
>>linkages found in 'small' software by adding speed-absorbing layers of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>API can be said to contain much in the way of abstraction.

The NT kernel is isolated from hardware by a Hardware Abstraction
Layer (HAL). NT 3 & 4 have run on DEC Alpha, MIPS, PowerPC, SPARC and
(rumored) IBM mainframe. That means Office and other applications
written to the NT API ran on those platforms.

Microsoft stopped targeting non-Intel processors in 1999 due to lack
of market demand. At the time, they had W2K running on Alpha and were
about to release it.

>>.. It speeds up Time To Market and extends the useful life
>>of the product. 
>
>"Useful life"??  It's revamped every ten minutes.  Rarely compatibly.
>:-)

Thanks for confirming my assertion about Time To Market. :)
```
