###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-28T12:23:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvvukf$13eu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b2efc$421fe664$45491f85$14707@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:b2efc$421fe664$45491f85$14707@KNOLOGY.NET...
> > Thus, my opinion is that the overriding error in the above example isn't
> > that the period is missing after "XYZ", it's that the end of the MOVE
> > statement is in Margin A.

> Your 2200 buddies agree with IBM.  :)

"Agrees with IBM" does not always imply "is more consistent with the COBOL
standard", though I acknowledge that in the context of pre-2002 COBOL format
there is an error here.

Whether you contend that a statement that immediately precedes a paragraph
header must in fact qualify additionally as a *sentence* (as would be the
case in free-form reference format anyway) or that what appears in Margin A
of the Procedure Division after a statement *or* a sentence has to be either
a paragraph header or a section header, the source code I cited isn't
standard pre-2002 COBOL.   I think the first rule takes precedence over the
second.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-26T10:29:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>I very much enjoyed reading Bill Klein's take on the '02 standard.

It is worthwhile reading, as was your lengthy commentary.

> I agree, by the way, about thedangers of having user programs allocate
> memory.

It is also dangerous to give user programs the ability to start threads, put 
locks on data, and, in my experience it's dangerous to allow coders to write 
code =).....

Most memory allocation problems occur in embedded systems or server 
applications where there is, generally speaking, no upper limit to the life 
of the program.   This is not typical use of COBOL.

The other areas that have issues with memory management are libraries (like 
calling a C malloc to allocate, or concatenating strings) as it blurs the 
line on who is responsible for cleaning up the data.

I'm not trying to be funny, but what are the specific dangers you are 
worried about?

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-26T15:40:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oL0Ud.3460481$f47.621543@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
I think that he was talking about adding specific COBOL "ALLOCATE" and "FREE" 
statements available to "every" COBOL programmer (regardless of what their O/S 
level knowledge is).  It is true that many (most?) systems already have SOME way 
of doing this, it was never part of the "standard" COBOL language - before the 
'02 Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-28T10:48:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvvp21$vh2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...


> Most memory allocation problems occur in embedded systems or server
> applications where there is, generally speaking, no upper limit to the
life
> of the program.   This is not typical use of COBOL.

Maybe in your experience.  Many is the COBOL application I've seen on Unisys
MCP-based systems over the past thirty years that are designed around
run-forever programs handling transactions from an online systems, or as
libraries insulating a data base and providing a site-specific interface to
it, for its users.  Why should the program devoted to ATM processing ever
need to go to a *normal* end-of-job?

> The other areas that have issues with memory management are libraries
(like
> calling a C malloc to allocate, or concatenating strings) as it blurs the
> line on who is responsible for cleaning up the data.

> I'm not trying to be funny, but what are the specific dangers you are
> worried about?

For example, ALLOCATE in a program declared RECURSIVE with no corresponding
FREE.     Remember, I've been dealing with STACK OVERFLOW faults for some
time now, and I would prefer to *discourage* application designs that make
them (and their ilk) likely!  We recommend that COBOL programmers avoid
calling ALGOL routines that RESIZE the parameters (up or down) for analogous
reasons.

That isn't to say an application programmer can't make reasonable use of
such statements, but they'd better know what they're doing, and if they
don't do it right, the consequences are theirs to bear ... .

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-28T19:10:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<51KUd.3368335$B07.528845@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <cvvp21$vh2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In the IBM mainframe environment with CICS programs, it is a relatively common 
problem for application programs to do incorrect
   EXEC CICS GETMAIN
commands and to not correctly de-allocate such storage - or to try and address 
out-of the available storage. CICS is pretty good in "protecting" the 
environment from erroneous application programs, but this isn't to say that 
mis-use of this facility doesn't exist.
```

<a id="t-3a086748e0"></a>
## Thread: ANSI/ISO TR (technical reports) for COBOL - XML & (OO) Collection classses — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### ANSI/ISO TR (technical reports) for COBOL - XML & (OO) Collection classses

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T06:44:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ZBQd.2329988$f47.407311@news.easynews.com>`

```
To: comp.lang.cobol
       IBM-MAIN
       lngc distribution list
       Tek-Tips COBOL forum
         etc

J4 and WG4 (the ANSI and ISO-ish) COBOL groups are progressing with their 2nd 
and 3rd TRs (Technical Reports) to "expand"  the ISO 2002 COBOL Standard.  Both 
of these reports (similar but not quite the same as previous "Amendments") will 
be processed by ISO via "national body" comments.

If you are in the US (or deal with the US via COBOL activities) it is important 
that you get your comments on these drafts to J4

  >>> BY MARCH 21   <<<

so that J4 can process them at their meeting starting April 4 when determining 
the US position on the drafts.

Please send your comments to the J4 chair at:
   Don.Schricker <at > microfocus.com

The (OO) "Collections Classes" document has not yet been posted (after its 
revision at their most recent meeting).  However, you can check for it 
(occasionally) at:
   http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/index.asp?bhcp=1

and I will try and update "you all" when it does get posted.

The XML (via native COBOL source code) document is currently available at:

    http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/05-0027.doc

There is also an "historical" document showing what issues have been discussed 
(and resolve) at:

  http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/05-0028.doc

Unless you are into the "nitty-gritty" of how COBOL Standards work, I would 
suggest that you START looking at the 0027 document by looking at (for) the 
"CONCEPTS" section (with its example of how this syntax works).

   ***

A couple of suggestions (although everyone is welcomed to comment on whatever 
they want)

1) Do *not* include comments on the "whole standards process" when talking about 
a specific TR

2) Do not spend much time on what else (in addition) you think should be added 
(outside the "scope" of the current documents) *UNLESS* you think that missing 
some feature will make the entire feature "useless" (or un-implementable) - and 
if you think this is the situation  make a STRONG  case for why the entire TR 
should be delayed until the addition feature(s) are added. You *might* want to 
include something that you think should be placed into the "candidate for a 
future revision" if you think it might be missed otherwise, but don't spend a 
lot of time on this.

3) Catching typos and "minor" wording glitches is DEFINITELY welcomed.

4) If you think that the entire approach of one of the TR's is wrong or that the 
entire thing is "un-needed" then this is worth conveying to J4.  However, as the 
"international community" has already confirmed its desire for these features, 
you need to provide a detailed explanation of what you think is wrong about what 
is currently being done.

5) If you think that the document is "swell" and the best thing since sliced 
bread - but don't have any specific comments, then this is also worth 
communicating to J4 - so they get some idea of how many COBOL "users" think this 
is "worth the effort".

NOTE WELL:
  "gripping" in this forum (wherever you read this) and NOT communicating to J4 
is (IMHO) just plain silly (and a waste of time).  If you have an opinion, then 
let J4 know !!!

NOTE2:
  If you are NOT in the US or your COBOL is not US-based, then you should (also 
if you want to) convey your input to your specific national standards body.  For 
a complete list, see:

   http://www.iso.ch/iso/en/aboutiso/isomembers/MemberList.MemberSummary?MEMBERCODE=10

(So it doesn't get lost) You should reference the "TR" number (e.g.)

    ISO/IEC TR 24716:200x(E)
```

## Thread: Help with research — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with research

- **From:** "elena" <elena@monmouth.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T12:07:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108584473.675317.187460@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
Apologies for this off-topic post.

I'm a Java/C++ developer who is also studying psychology.

I would really appreciate it if you would complete a survey that I'm
using for a research project on programmers.

It's easy [Yes/No answers] and takes about 5 minutes.

I will be presenting the results at the American Psychological
Association convention in August.

The study link is:

                     http://www.elena.com

The survey measures "cognitive style" (analytical/intuitive) which
describes how you process information and learn. The people I've
pre-tested it with found it to be pretty interesting.

I can go to my friends, however it occurred to me that it might be
better to post in a newsgroup and get a larger, more diverse, and
random sample. 

Thanks again for your time, 

Elena
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with research

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@SpamStopper.Org>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T18:00:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VqmdnROTLJsTQ47fRVn_vg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1108584473.675317.187460@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
> Apologies for this off-topic post.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe I am being over analytical but starting with the very first
question the survey is ambiguous. What is the context of this survey?
Are you trying to find out how programmers approach business programming and work?
Are you trying to find out how programmers approach general life decisions?
Are you trying to find out if programmers approach life decisions differently
than non-programmers?

Take the first question:
In my experience, rational thought is the only realistic basis for making decisions.

In a business programming development work context the answer is yes.
In life decisions such as who should I marry or what church should I go to the answer is no.
In a general decision like what car should I buy the answer is maybe.

You need to frame your survey better.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with research

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T16:38:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108600708.776796.187780@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
> Take the first question:
> In my experience, rational thought is the only realistic basis for
> making decisions.

Not only, that but everyone usually thinks that their own decisions are
based on 'rational thought' regardless of what others may think of
those decisions.

> decisions such as who should I marry

Excuse me, but is that a decision that _I_ could have made ? I wish I'd
known that sooner (or maybe not).

> or what church should I go to

Now that was _entirely_ rational on my part, and made when I was 7.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with research

- **From:** "elena" <elena@monmouth.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-16T20:49:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108615796.722797.193290@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1108584473.675317.187460@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <VqmdnROTLJsTQ47fRVn_vg@giganews.com>`

```
You are correct. Current research is looking at how cognitive style may
differ depending on the task at hand. The instrument that I'm using is
well-known, well-tested, and mature. However, its developers view
cognitive style as a unitary dimension, whereas other researchers
believe that it is multi-dimensional, and requires a much more complex
framework (and more complex and lengthy tests). I chose to use a
unitary instrument in this study for simplicity's sake. I couldn't
imagine that someone would spend more than a few minutes to complete a
test for someone they don't even know. Maybe I was wrong?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with research

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T02:13:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%uXQd.7813$dZ.533567@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108615796.722797.193290@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1108584473.675317.187460@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <VqmdnROTLJsTQ47fRVn_vg@giganews.com> <1108615796.722797.193290@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
elena wrote:
> You are correct. Current research is looking at how cognitive style may
> differ depending on the task at hand. The instrument that I'm using is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Perhaps.  I found the exact same problem ... many of the questions had 
to be interpeted as to context.  A completely opposite answer would 
apply depending on the setting ...  I tend to approach problems with my 
cat differently than those with my computer. Rationality is not logical 
with a cat, only temper tantrums work. Temper tantrums over a computer 
problem ... well after the first twenty-five years they become rare, if 
only based on their futility.

On the other hand, I started filling it out during a compile. I may not 
have finished it if it had taken lot longer.

Donald
```

```
"elena" <elena@monmouth.com> wrote in message 
news:1108584473.675317.187460@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com...
> Apologies for this off-topic post.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Elena

I scored a 42, about in the middle.  A couple of times in school I took an exam 
to see if one is left or right-hemisphere dominated in their thinking, and both 
times scored a zero.  So maybe brain hemisphere dominance determines analytical 
vs intuitive preference in one's thinking?  I can't draw a conclusion on 
this -since I'm so balanced, I suffer analysis paralysis  :)

-Fred
```

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M Klein)
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T05:21:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1108617674@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-modified: 2004/10/27
Version: 3.03
URL: http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm 
Maintainer: wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M Klein)
  
                                COBOL FAQ
                      (Frequently Asked Questions)
    
    Last-Modified: Spring 2002
    
    The full (somewhat out-of-date) COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:
    
    http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm > 
   
    If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, 
    please send private email to:
    
        wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
   
   
    This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):
   
     - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
     - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
     - Where can I contact... ?
     - What about OO COBOL? 
     - Books about COBOL
     - COBOL Tools
     - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
     - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
     - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education? 
 		Tutorials? Etc
   
   	 and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Compiler for old Linux — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler for old Linux

- **From:** jr411035@yahoo.de (Johannes)
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T05:09:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com>`

```
Got an account on an old Linux box with kernel version 2.0.30. I want
my sysop to install a cobol compiler on that machine. He says there is
none available running on that system at a resonable prize.

Though I don't wanna give up yet. Probably you know one?
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler for old Linux

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-17T10:07:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108663653.720376.148220@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com>`

```
> kernel version 2.0.30.

I've seen more modern version in museums ;-)

Actually I do have a couple of machine here running 2.0.xx kernel
because it runs nicely on old 486s or P100s using it for a Freesco
firewall, LRP print server, or PocketLinux for a terminal server.  I
was running a 386 20MHz as my Freesco firewall and gateway but it broke
and was upgraded to a P133.

You could try using OpenCobol or TinyCobol, which are free, but you
will probably find that they require a 2.4 kernel.

Is there any particular reason why you haven't loaded a more recent
version of Linux ?  Its like you are running MS-DOS 3.1 and want to run
Win32 software.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler for old Linux

- **From:** jr411035@yahoo.de (Johannes)
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T06:28:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9804f7e9.0502180628.376a96b6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com> <1108663653.720376.148220@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote :
>> kernel version 2.0.30.
> 
> I've seen more modern version in museums ;-)

;-)

> You could try using OpenCobol or TinyCobol, which are free, but you
> will probably find that they require a 2.4 kernel.

Yes, that's the problem of OpenCobol.

> Is there any particular reason why you haven't loaded a more recent
> version of Linux ?

Several, yes. First, it's not my computer ;-) Second, there are lots
of individual modifications done to system programs over the years by
different programmers. Very hard to update without losing them.

> Its like you are running MS-DOS 3.1 and want to run Win32 software.

Well, the is the IBM System/36 for example, old and slow, but capable
of compiling cobol sources and running them ;-) So I guess cobol does
not necessarily be too much for an ancient environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler for old Linux

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-18T10:38:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108751937.525486.185140@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9804f7e9.0502180628.376a96b6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com> <1108663653.720376.148220@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <9804f7e9.0502180628.376a96b6@posting.google.com>`

```
> but capable of compiling cobol sources and running them ;-)

Because someone actually developed a Cobol compiler for that system.
If no one developed a Cobol compiler for Linux 2.0 kernel then you
cannot have one.  As a counter example the iMac is a modern machine but
(afaik) there is no Cobol compiler for it.

There is no correlation between 'ancient' or 'modern' and 'Cobol
available', only between 'someone implemented it' and 'Cobol
available'.

Of course the source for OpenCobol and TinyCobol and all the software
that it depends on is available so you could always try to implement it
yourself.

> First, it's not my computer ;-)

Linux has a very low resource requirement.  You should be able to find
an old 500MHz or so machine in a dumpster that bloated Windows would
stagger on but would be perfectly usable with a recent Linux loaded
from a free CD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler for old Linux

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jr411035@yahoo.de (Johannes)
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T02:31:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9804f7e9.0502210231.57233b66@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9804f7e9.0502170509.71ef3b43@posting.google.com> <1108663653.720376.148220@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <9804f7e9.0502180628.376a96b6@posting.google.com> <1108751937.525486.185140@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> If no one developed a Cobol compiler for Linux 2.0 kernel then you
> cannot have one.

So you don't know. Why are you then trying to proselytize me?

There are compilers running on this kernel, but both I found so far
were far too expensive for me. That's why I were asking if someone
knows another one.

I were not asking if I should buy another machine, install another
kernel or anything else.
```

```
get one of them working.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<9804f7e9.0502210227.3ceedde4@posting.google.com>`

```
The year is 10,005 AD. The Unification Wars are over, and the colonies
of the galaxy know independence. But such a peace can never last. Now
a new war is coming, one that will shake the universe. In this
turn-based game of resource management and warfare, you will have the
opportunity to manage your colonies, research new technologies, and
attempt to take over the farflung sectors of space. Sign up now and
join the game for free and play with thousands of players around the
globe.
```

```
Lueko Willms wrote:
>    Better to work to stop the wars taking place _now_ and to prevent
> the wars being announced (US-wars against Iran, Korea, what have you),
> and preventing the world to be converted in colonies again.

Uh, no. First, the United States has never had an official "colony," in 
contradistinction to, for example, 19th-century Germany. If we were into 
colonization, Germany (again, just as an example), or at least the western 
part, would be one.

Second, we take national leaders as we find them. We remove them from power, 
kill their children, exile their families, evict them from their homes, 
confiscate their property and money, and throw their skanky asses in a dank 
cell where they fill their meaningless days contemplating imminent 
execution, followed by cremation and scattered ashes. We take no chances.

The U.S. is democracy in its purest form: By free and popular vote we 
elected a leader to undertake the above actions toward any mope that even 
squints in our direction.

Just watch: France, Lebanon, and the EC are going to get a pissing-contest 
with Syria over Syria's occupation of Lebanon. Iran will come to Syria's 
aid, and two more terroristic, tyrannical regimes will go pop. North Korea 
will soon reach the point where even its army is starving and they'll do 
something silly. Poof! Re-unification. (Like Germany, only worse.)

We live in interesting times.
```

```
> Hello all,

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Diba

I'm not a mainframer, but unless somebody tells you to the contrary, you 
might check out amazon.com for Sams "COBOL Unleashed". First published 
in '98 - so it may no longer be available. But check Amazon under 
'Second-hand books'. (Could also try www.samspublishing.com).

both JCL and VSAM.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
> Hello all,
> I am new to Mainframe. Can anyone suggest some URL's from where i can
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Diba


Hello Diba,

Thinking helps, you must have been able to see the list of earlier
posts to this newsgroup and been able to see the item labelled COBOL
FAQ a few lines down!

This is written by Bill Klein and though not updated very recently is
still highly relevant.  Among many other items, it contains links to
the major suppliers of COBOL compilers, most of whom, including IBM and
Microsoft, have all/most of their manuals and supplementary reading
online.

With regards,

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need e-books on Mainframe topics

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-20T11:26:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108927571.667754.268110@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d9sg111hr0vc1ons10ucvfo6lgrr3jriqj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com> <1108841503.998277.222370@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <d9sg111hr0vc1ons10ucvfo6lgrr3jriqj@4ax.com>`

```
> Microsoft dropped Cobol in the '80s.

No.  Microsoft Cobol 5.0 was dated 1993.  Fixpacks were issued in 1994.


It wasn't that MS dropped Cobol but that MicroFocus took away the
franchise when they opened a US sales office.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need e-books on Mainframe topics

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Yg9FCpHpv+Rf092yn@visi.com>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol, dibalok@gmail.com (Diba) spake unto us, saying:

>Hello all,
>I am new to Mainframe. Can anyone suggest some URL's from where i can
>get ebooks on VSAM, JCL, COBOL.......

While I'm a UNIVAC/Unisys mainframer and not an IBM'er, I've seen a
number of good books on IBM mainframe topics at www.half.com.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need e-books on Mainframe topics

- **From:** "Steve.T" <steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-19T20:49:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108874963.166468.280310@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com>`

```
What, exactly, are you looking for for VSAM, JCL, .....

The COBOL side, most have already assumed you are looking for books to
learn it.

If you are looking to learn VSAM, JCL, TSO, ISPF, etc., there are books
for this. But if you are looking for the books from IBM on their
systems, you will find that at
http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/zseries/zos/bkserv/
I will assume once you are in the library you will be able to find the
manuals you are seeking.

Later,
Steve.T
```

#### ↳ Re: Need e-books on Mainframe topics

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-20T08:26:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mRXRd.58055$Th1.12586@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com>`

```


Diba wrote:
> Hello all,
> I am new to Mainframe. Can anyone suggest some URL's from where i can
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Diba

Well, these aren't ebooks and they aren't free, but Murach offers 
books on CICS, COBOL, JCL, DB2, and VSAM.  I know programmers who rely 
on their books and they have been in business for decades.

http://www.murach.com/index.htm

http://www.murach.com/books/mcb2/index.htm

http://www.murach.com/books/zjcl/index.htm

http://www.murach.com/books/vsmr/index.htm

http://www.murach.com/books/vsmx/index.htm

Good luck!
```

#### ↳ Re: Need e-books on Mainframe topics

- **From:** pv+usenet@pobox.com (Paul Vader)
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T21:37:59
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<111n9hnfreiocc9@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ec51a235.0502190129.6a500dc0@posting.google.com>`

```
dibalok@gmail.com (Diba) writes:
>Hello all,
>I am new to Mainframe. Can anyone suggest some URL's from where i can
>get ebooks on VSAM, JCL, COBOL.......

Just about every IBM manual is available online via bookmanager or PDF.
Just go here: http://www.ibm.com/support/publications/us/library/

I'm not saying that's a good way to learn from scratch (IBM's manuals are
an acquired taste), but it is what you asked for... *
```

## Thread: US-MD-Rockville: Lawson Developer (MicroFocus COBOL & Web) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### US-MD-Rockville: Lawson Developer (MicroFocus COBOL & Web)

- **From:** "Newsgroup Poster" <Newsgroup _Poster@n_o_s_p_a_m.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-19T16:46:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,alt.comp.cobol.cancelbots,alt.comp.lang.cobol,balt.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,va.jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<yNqdnexT3oAELorfRVn-rA@comcast.com>`

```
Montgomery County Public Schools (MCPS), Rockville, MD, provides 137,000
students a world-class education. A stable, collegial, professional
workforce applies
cutting-edge technology to support classrooms and offices in a suburban
Washington, DC community rich in educational, cultural and social
opportunities. Our mission provides a wonderful opportunity to make a
difference.

MCPS provides excellent benefits, which is comprised of the following
components: medical, dental, vision, prescription drug coverage, a
retirement plan, flexible spending accounts, tax-deferred annuity (TDA)
403(b) and 457 plans, long term health care and life. Please e-mail cover
letter and resume to: Mrs. Dale S. Horos: Dale_Horos@mcpsmd.org



Application Developer II
Candidate will provide application development and support for the Lawson
HR suite.

Candidate must have:
� Minimum of five years of software development experience.
� Program design and development skills using COBOL or MicroFocus COBOL.
� Strong technical and functional knowledge of business applications
� Bachelor degree from an accredited college or university.

Other skills desired and considered:
� Experience with the support or Lawson business application products
including writing custom programs using Lawson Cobol.
� Experience in developing web base applications
� JAVAScript, HTML, Lawson Design Studio, SQL
� Knowledge of C#, .Net and Rational UML
� Report Writers such as Crystal or Discoverer

The person in this position will work as a lead application development
expert working with end users, programmers and other team members to
develop solutions to business and technical requirements. Duties include
requirement collection, development of complex functional and technical
specifications, analysis, design, coding, testing, implementation and
technical support. This position is also expected to direct and mentor
other programmers.
```

```
determine rates.
```

## Thread: hp-ux compiler — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### hp-ux compiler

- **From:** "Harrie_Nak" <harrie@nak.nl>
- **Date:** 2005-02-19T22:50:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c80f3$4217b4a6$3e3baf90$511@news.versatel.nl>`

```
Hi,
I am a newbe with Cobol. I want to learn more about programming in Cobol, 
but I hear people say that my compiler will not work on my hp9000 with hp-ux 
11.
Is this correct, and if so, is there an affordable solution for this 
problem?
Any help will be appreciated.
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: hp-ux compiler

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-19T14:06:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108850815.465894.163130@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c80f3$4217b4a6$3e3baf90$511@news.versatel.nl>`
- **References:** `<c80f3$4217b4a6$3e3baf90$511@news.versatel.nl>`

```
> I hear people say that my compiler will not work on my hp9000 with
hp-ux 11.  Is this correct,

As you haven't said what 'my compiler' is then it is not possible to
say whether it is correct or not.  Is it one that you wrote ?  or one
that you run on a PC ?

The HP9000 is not an Intel PC.  HPUX 11 is not Windows. If 'my
compiler' is not specifically for HP9000 and HPUX 11 then, no, it won't
work.

> and if so, is there an affordable solution for this problem?

Probably not.  You could try to get OpenCobol or TinyCobol compiled to
run on HPUX, but it may take a lot of work.
```

```
Serious response to a semi-humorous question.

This newsgroup includes (but is not limited to) a number  of people who make 
their LIVINGS (directly or indirectly) from the sale of software and/or the 
development of software.

Therefore, when someone posts to the newsgroup asking for a solution REQUIRING 
the theft of software (intellectual property) this usually (often) gets a pretty 
cold response.
```

```
Thanks, at least someone with a normal answer to a normal question.
I don't think it is strange or even criminal to try some things at home 
without any intention to break any laws.
I don't think that I am the only living person on this planet with this 
idea.
I mean I do the same thing with for example Oracle (free downloadable for 
test and training purposes). I don't see any differences in using Cobol. 
(Except of course in the way it is distributed etc., so no remarks on this 
issue pls). Eventualy my 'experiments' can, and probably will, lead to 
selling more licenses. If this is not possible I will look for alternatives. 
In that case everyone will loose both HP and Micro Focus.

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: hp-ux compiler

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Message-ID:** `<1109103271.669636.67200@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

- **References:** `<c80f3$4217b4a6$3e3baf90$511@news.versatel.nl> <69b11$4217c043$3e3baf90$11771@news.versatel.nl> <1108853668.498646.12460@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <4788e$421a2002$3e3baf90$13172@news.versatel.nl> <cvd87n$a8k$1@panix5.panix.com> <7498c$421b8ead$3e3baf90$10603@news.versatel.nl>`

```
> I don't think it is strange or even criminal to try some things at
home
> without any intention to break any laws.

In many cases it _is_ criminal, and the user may well be intending to
break laws.

> I don't think that I am the only living person on this planet with
this idea.

Some think that they should be able to rob banks or liquor stores.
Some think it not strange to scam money off old people.

> I mean I do the same thing with for example Oracle.

If they offer a free version then that is fine.  It seemed that you
didn't want to ask HP because you _knew_ that 'your compiler' was not
legitimate.  You didn't want to say which compiler it was because you
knew you would be told it was stealing. You _intended_ to steal
software.

> Eventualy my 'experiments' can, and probably will, lead to
> selling more licenses.

Or, if you can break the licensing scheme, to more copies being stolen.

> In that case everyone will loose both HP and Micro Focus.

After several attempts at finding out what your 'my compiler' is you
finally reveal that it may be MicroFocus.

Perhaps you don't know that there are several different brands of
compilers.  Perhaps you are unaware that each will have their own
mechanisms.  You asked for help and did not provide sufficient
information for us to supply any.

If you contact MF you may find they offer academic and student
editions.
```

```
for your amusement, I hope

Newsgroups: comp.lang.fortran
From: bv <b...@Xsdynamix.com> - Find messages by this author
Date: Fri, 18 Feb 2005 21:11:08 GMT
Local: Fri, Feb 18 2005 1:11 pm
Subject: Evolution of a Programmer
Reply | Reply to Author | Forward | Print | Individual Message | Show
original | Report Abuse

A preview sampler of the f2003 "craft" appears to be under "masters"
category... enjoy!

** High School/Jr.High

>   10 PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
>   20 END

** First year in College

>   program Hello(input, output)
>     begin
>       writeln('Hello World')
>     end.

** Senior year in College

- Hide quoted text -
- Show quoted text -

>   (defun hello
>     (print
>       (cons 'Hello (list 'World))))

** New professional

>   #include <stdio.h>
>   void main(void)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   }

** Seasoned professional

- Hide quoted text -
- Show quoted text -

>   #include <iostream.h>
>   #include <string.h>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>   };

>     return(stream << s.ptr);
>   }

>   {
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   }

>   int main()
>   {
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   }

world."?
```

<a id="t-dde7eb5268"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus Netexpress vs APS — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus Netexpress vs APS

- **From:** "Caederus" <davidburnham@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-20T18:41:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108953664.479316.78020@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
I currently work in a Micro Focus Netexpress shop and I have an
interview for a job with a firm that is an Micro Focus APS shop. In
preparation I have started to review the APS documentation on
www.microfocus.com. My question is what similarities/differences jump
out to those who may have used both?

Thanks,

David
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Netexpress vs APS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T03:57:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X_cSd.2904984$f47.508449@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1108953664.479316.78020@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
A) have you worked with ANY "application generator"?  If not, the APS will be a 
"different way of working"

B) In Mainframe Express, Enterprise Edition, APS and "traditional" Mainframe 
express are "integrated".  You probably want to know what release of MF they are 
on (and/or going to)
```

---

<a id="t-e194aaefbd"></a>
## Thread: Help with internationalization (i18n) — 6 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with internationalization (i18n)

- **From:** ed.fuentetaja@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T03:04:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1108983114.190101.121120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi,

My company needs to adapt a Cobol/CICS system developed in-house to the
foreign market. We are still evaluating the number of maps that need to
be translated to English (from Spanish). It's probably gonna be large.

Anyone knows of any good tools, products, or consulting firms who could
help us in this task? I am thinking of some kind of tool that extracts
literals from maps and replaces them by their translated version. We
already have a staff of qualified translators.

I'd appreciate any help,
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with internationalization (i18n)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T19:47:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bVqSd.457304$Xk.132660@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1108983114.190101.121120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1108983114.190101.121120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
ed.fuentetaja@gmail.com wrote:
> Hi,
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
At a guess, wouldn't have thought there would be any obvious resource 
for this. So some off the cuff suggestions.

1. Do a google - you will have to be fairly 'clever' on your word 
searches, "internalization", "atlas", "maps", "Spanish", "English", 
"translation" and various combinations of those sort of key words.

2. Not sure where you are located but "fuentetaja' to an 'Anglo' does 
seem to indicate Spanish origin ? So contact as appropriate your Spanish 
or UK/US embassy or consulate - see if they can point you in the right 
direction.

3. Now that the EU embraces some 40 (?) languages with the inclusion of 
the new members from the old Soviet bloc, each document has to be 
translated for each member country. Maybe there's some secretariat in 
the EU that you could quiz, or your local country's EU branch if you are 
a European. (Seriously - you could give the CIA a shot - they aren't 
solely cloak and dagger ! They keep a watching brief on international 
politics/economics, and do have Web topics that you and I can access).

4. Pick and shovel approach - and hardly desired I'm sure. Use your 
translators with a Spanish atlas using the index to create a lookup 
table in English. (I'd be willing to bet this feature exists within the EU).

Your request for info kind of reminds me of something that occurred here 
some years back. Poor old Jose in Portugal was having problems with Net 
  Express. So I wrote to him using Babelfish which did a 'fractured' 
translation into Portuguese. Jose was impressed, thinking at last he had 
found a soul mate. We got nowhere. Subsequently somebody from Brazil 
jumped into help. The Brazilian's final comments about Babelfish, "Oh my 
God. It isn't Portuguese and it isn't English either !"  -:)


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Ira Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-27T14:23:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvt9si0j6e@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1108983114.190101.121120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
<ed.fuentetaja@gmail.com> wrote in message
news:1108983114.190101.121120@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> My company needs to adapt a Cobol/CICS system developed in-house to the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'd appreciate any help,

The DMS Software Reengineering toolkit could be used to help do this.
DMS is a tool for making massive, regular changes to source code.
It understands the target language the same way the compiler does.
DMS could be configured to extract all the literal strings from you
code, and put them in a dictionary to be translated; it could
then be used to replace the original strings with the translated strings,
and/or replace the original strings with a dictionary lookup.

See http://www.semanticdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
DMS is available with an IBM COBOL front end.
```

```
I have coded some lines

 
 copy-file:

 UPRO-upro-name1               procedure-pointer EXTERNAL.
 UPRO-upro-name2               procedure-pointer EXTERNAL.
 ... 
      
 Upro-Set-Pointer:

 Z-UPRO                          PIC X(8).

 ''INIT-ROUTINE  before all Calls''
                                         
 MOVE 'upro-name1'               TO       Z-UPRO
 SET  UPRO-name1                 TO ENTRY Z-UPRO
 
                                                
 MOVE 'upro-name2'               TO       Z-UPRO
 SET  UPRO-name1                 TO ENTRY Z-UPRO

 ....

 Upro Call Pointer:

 Call  UPRO-name1       using something


 I have two question. why get I  an error, if the Load-module isn't
there in the  UPRO-SET-POINTER Modul in the INIT-ROUTINE. I thought
the first time I  CALL     the UPRO-XYZ   I Link the Modul
Upro-nameXXX  and not in the   SET  command.

 The next question is, why get I an  0C4 on a customer-system , in the

          SET UPRO-NameXX    to    ENTRY Z-UPRO

 All Load-Modules are there.

 thanks 
 joerg
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure-pointer OS/390 and 0C4

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T22:03:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-8304DF.22033622022005@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<7f6b3f56.0502210834.58d69db4@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <7f6b3f56.0502210834.58d69db4@posting.google.com>,
 Joerg.Brehe@set-software.de (j?rg brehe) wrote:

> I have coded some lines
> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>  joerg


The SET/TO ENTRY will cause an error if the module isn't available 
because the SET of a procedure pointer will try to load the module, 
initialized the runtime if necessary, create a new instance if 
reentrent, etc.

It will do everything a call would except transfer control to the first 
instruction at the entry point.

This is troublesome.  With the CALL verb, you could use ON OVERFLOW or 
ON EXCEPTION to trap a missing module.  With SET/ENTRY you cannot.

Perhaps the only way I know of to prevent the S806 is with an LE 
condition handler.

As to the SOC4 on the clients system -- well, I have no clue on that.  
It could be just about anything...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Procedure-pointer OS/390 and 0C4

- **Date:** 2005-02-23T05:25:36-08:00

- **References:** `<7f6b3f56.0502210834.58d69db4@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8304DF.22033622022005@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Statement in the INIT-ROUTINE and no 0C4 occurs, then I have compiled
this modul Upro202 with the new compiler and put the SET statement
again in my source, and it works again well, no 0C4. I don't need this
modul, so i kill this old modul.

thanks for the above information. 

jï¿½rg
```

```
jd.smithuk@talk21.com wrote:
> I had a need for a small application for my own use and this seemed
> the perfect and fully licenced tool. Installaed and all running great
> apart from I cant generate anything if I use screens anyone any ideas?

Never used it, although I think I have a copy lurking somewhere. Can you 
be a bit more specific as to what is happening. In theory, using Micro 
Focus, this one-line program should compile and run :-

display "Hello World".

In a Windows environment the one-liner should appear as a white on black 
'DOS Text' window. Does it compile for you and what happens ? Are you 
just using straight ANSI accepts/displays like the one-liner above or 
are you using Screen Section ?

Point of interest - you could get Net Express University Edition, which 
  is a 'chopped down' version of the main Version 4.0, for $100 US - 
then you've got all the whistles and bells. 'Chopped down' - from memory 
any one source is limited to something like 2,300 (or is that 23,000) 
lines of code. It's the full blown 'animal' with that limitation - 
intended not to tempt you into distributing and trying to by-pass M/F's 
runtime fees.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> jd.smithuk@talk21.com wrote:
> 
>> I had a need for a small application for my own use and this seemed
>> the perfect and fully licenced tool. Installaed and all running great
>> apart from I cant generate anything if I use screens anyone any ideas?

A follow-up on my previous. Can't find the diskette but I have the book 
'Elements of OO COBOL" (First Edition), by Wilson Price. There's a 
'sleeve' at the back, which may or may not have included that diskette.
(It was '97 when he sent me the book - so do I actually have the 
diskette ? Dunno). There's an order form which says you can order 
Personal COBOL for Windows 3.1. V1.1 - but it doesn't seem to indicate a 
Version # for the COBOL - and you are talking about V 2.

Just to double check - does yours have an ANIMATOR and the ability to do 
OO, (Object Orientation), as well. Have you faithfully followed the 
Installation instructions and are you aware of the significance of ADIS 
if using screens. (ADIS - "A" = "Accept" and "DIS" = "Display"). If I've 
thrown you for a loop on the latter check out the Help on ADIS, ADISCTRL 
etc., because you have to make settings, plus include a reference to the 
COBOL pathname in your autoexec.bat - normally the latter occurs as a 
result of your installation setup.

Post the code for a program you were trying to run - and confirm are you 
correctly setup for ADIS.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
the generated code ?

the file is getting written somewhere that you don't expect, the
COBDIR= path or where the executables are.
```

```
If you want to continue to use an older COBOL compiler for MS-DOS 
under Windows XP, have you considered running it in a DOS emulator.

Here's a free one, although it was originally written for running DOS 
games:

http://dosbox.sourceforge.net/news.php?show_news=1

I was able to run the education version of Realia COBOL, a crippled 
version of RealiaCOBOL 3.0 from about 1990, in DOSBOX 6.3.  In a WinXP 
dos box the compiler crashes when attempting to write data to the DOS 
screen by poking data into fixed memory locations.  That no longer 
works under Windows XP.

I hope that helps.
```

```
jd.smithuk@talk21.com wrote:
> On Mon, 21 Feb 2005 21:08:16 GMT, jd.smithuk@talk21.com wrote:
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Got you. Just want to suck it and see. Don't want to spend any money.

Richard mentioned Screens.exe - that meant nothing to me. There's no 
reference to it in Net Express - perhaps he knows something I don't know 
-  although that wouldn't be unusual :-). Arnold helped in the confusion 
by throwing in the reference to using an XP :-)

First off, recent thought I had. Yours is only on one diskette ? If even 
all zipped, it suggests a pretty much 'cut-down' version.

So my Dear Dr. Watson, with me playing Sherlock Holmes :-

1. Does it have an Animator feature to debug the code
2. Was my question about ADIS relevant
3. What entries do you have in autoexec.bat, i.e., COBOL pathanmes and 
in the case of ADIS, from memory, there should be an entry in 
config.sys, perhaps
4. Does it specify Screen Section and does it include Micro Focus 
routines for handling screen attributes.
5. I see you used the word 'manual' - what manual are you referring to - 
one that came with the diskette.
6. You said some 'old' programs worked. What old programs ? Some you had 
from somewhere else or are they M/F examples provided with the diskette. 
Did you just run them or did you re-compile first.
7. When you compile, what summary message are you getting from Micro Focus.

The list could go on and on, but get the idea ?

As regards my original 'Hello', try this :-


working-storage section.
01 charx	pic x.

Procedure Division.
display 'Hello1'
accept charx
display 'Hello2'
accept charx
.........
.........
repeat several times.

Now step through it in Animator mode to see what it does and you should
be prompted for each 'accept charx'.

Wouldn't hurt to post one of those 'old programs', which works.

Let's get beyond guessing and get specific :-)

Ahh yes. You wanted to do this 'to prove a point to a friend'. What 
point - perhaps somebody here can back up 'your point' - that would save 
you buggering around with the compiler :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-bafec10d6b"></a>
## Thread: Can you wakeUp cobol please?... — 11 msgs

### Can you wakeUp cobol please?...

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T13:36:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

I have catch-22 dilemma and need your kind help, again.  I made
a simple graphical window program I will call "Parent", the parent
will launch a console-window (characterBase) program I will call
"child", by using the win32 API "CreateProcess" function, and will
Wait for this program INFINITELY by using the win32 API function
"WaitForSingleObject", until it returns control back to the parent.

the child needs to send a message to the parent during its active
execution time  and request a service operation, for example:
(generate & sort a text file, or launch a GUI-Graphical Program).
however, when the child is active --- the parent is hibernating in
the background in its waiting-state, until it receives the return
code from the child program along with the returned OS control.

My Dilemma? how can I wakeup the parent to respond to the message
it will receive from the child while keeping the status quo??

the workaround is for the child to exit with a special ReturnCode,
and have the parent respond to that request accordingly, and then
the parent would Re-Launch back again the child program so it can
continue its Previous Process --- however, when the child gets
Re-Launched, the end-user will not be able to pickup from where
he or she leftoff the last operation with the child.  Also, Only
the parent program is capabl of launching a Graphical Window
program by using the win32 API "CreateProcess" function.

I am hoping that you have a wakeup call for cobol or me.
thanks a lot for the generous help, Kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can you wakeUp cobol please?...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-21T22:23:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WbtSd.34738$by5.23361@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1109022179.254443.235370@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1109023679.089341.194510@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:1109023679.089341.194510@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...

> I made a simple graphical window program I will call "Parent", the parent
>will launch a console-window (characterBase) program I will call
>"child", by using the win32 API "CreateProcess" function, and will
>Wait for this program INFINITELY by using the win32 API function
>"WaitForSingleObject", until it returns control back to the parent.

>the child needs to send a message to the parent during its active
>execution time  and request a service operation, for example:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>code from the child program along with the returned OS control

[added later] [but you shouldn't have, because this is absolutely possible.
Not too wise, but 'do-able' nonetheless]
> if the callable program is a GUI-program like a
> ListView window, the child is NOT capable of
> calling or launching it either.

The parent program must be multi-threaded: One thread to do nothing but
launch the child and wait for it to complete; one thread to service requests
(which I would trigger with named event, also in a WaitForSingleObject or
WaitForMultipleObjects Loop); and of course, your main thread servicing the
parent's GUI window(s), which must be disabled during the execution of the
child process. You can, of course, have the main thread of 'parent' handle
the service requests, but that gets a little trickier (you need to use a
MsgWaitForMultipleObjects loop isnteaqd of a 'standard' Windows message
loop, call, or arrange for the child to do virtual allocation of memory in
the parent's address space), so let's forget about those options when
multi-threading  is available.

Or, take the easy way out: instead of using CreateProcess to launch another
program, write the 'child' program as a DLL and just call it from within the
process space of 'parent.'  No muss, no fuss, no bother. Control is sure a
lot easier this way (service requests become a simple CALL). .

If your compiler does not support multi-threaded programs and/or creation of
dynamic link libraries, uh, er, not sure what to do next, except perhaps (he
shamelessly added) ..If you really need this, I can create something like
this on a professional basis.
```

- **References:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1109022179.254443.235370@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1109023679.089341.194510@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <WbtSd.34738$by5.23361@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Hello Michael,

in case you are wondering about the cobol wakeUp call:
when I compiled the file (child) as a .DLL file, the Net Express
cobol compiler was NOT able to resolve the entry-point call and gave
me an error messgae that said :
"error LNK2001:unresolved external symbol DisplayAboutScreen@4"
and when I included the file (child) in the cobol project and compiled
it as an .OBJ file, the entry-point call was resolved fine ---
However,
the parent program did NOT wait for the child to complete it job first,
before it re-claimed control back from the call to the child. So, I am
back to square one with the win32 API "CreateProcess" function. In
this case here, I need the parent to wait for the child until the end
of its job cycle ---  and just like I said before,  its a catch-22. :-)

thanks a lot, Kellie.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can you wakeUp cobol please?...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T14:10:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y3HSd.35076$by5.33733@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1109022179.254443.235370@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1109023679.089341.194510@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <WbtSd.34738$by5.23361@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1109043245.791839.246970@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:1109043245.791839.246970@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
> Hello Michael,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> of its job cycle ---  and just like I said before,  its a catch-22. :-)

Since I don't know the compiler, I could be way off base here, but.....

If you were able to link the DLL, either at compile time (an OBJ file
handled by the linker) or at program load time (the way the Windows system
DLLs are linked), then you don't need any "WaitFor" functions except if you
call an external process (another EXE) from somewhere... the ENTRYs in your
DLL are simply called with a CALL statement and your program WILL NOT
continue until that called procedure (ENTRY) has completed.

If the procedure in your DLL is in turn calling other ENTRYs in your 'main'
code, the same rule applies.

Therefore, if you have some code continuing when it shouldn't, it's because
it's coded that way. How you'd code that is unknown to me because AFAIK that
can't be done. (Unless you are calling an external with CreateProcess and
not waiting for it?).

I mean your entire program should be something like

 * MAIN
  MAIN.
      Other stuff
      ......
      CALL   My-Entry-in-my-dll  USING whatever RETURNING whatever
     .....

      EXIT PROGRAM or GOBACK or however you end a program

====
* DLL
  ENTRY My-ENTRY-In-my-dll
    USING  whatever
    yadda, yadda
   * ***************************
   * call an entry in main module
   * *************************
   CALL ENTRY-IN-MAIN USING whatever
   do other stuff
   * ***************************
   * call external program
   * *************************
   Call CreateProcess (parameters)
   Call WaitForSingleObject (process handle returned in ProcessInformation
Structure parameter passed to createProcess).


But as I said, I don't know the compiler, and I am not sure how OBJ files
are handled. Someone else here will know that. But the above is a 'generic'
program using a DLL, calling both external programs and ENTRYs in the
'parent' (in this case 'parent' is a misnomer) program..

This DOES assume that MAIN is compiled with correct options and properly
written to avoid any reentrancy problems.

MCM
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>

- **References:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1109022179.254443.235370@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1109023679.089341.194510@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <WbtSd.34738$by5.23361@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <1109043245.791839.246970@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <y3HSd.35076$by5.33733@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Hello,

thanks a lot for All the feedback and the great help from everyone.

I have found a win32 API at the MSDN that will wakeUp cobol with
a jolt of a large cappuccion --- it looks devilishly clever to solve my
catch-22 dilemma. Below is the code that I need to work with.

Regards, Kellie.

*>--- source code  ------>*

   call winapi "CreateSemaphoreA" using
           by value 0 size 4
           by value 4 size 4
           by value 4 size 4
           by reference lpszReadSem
        returning       hSemRead
    end-call.

    call winapi "OpenSemaphoreA" using
          by value     SEMAPHORE-ALL-ACCESS
          by value     0 size 4
          by reference lpszReadSem
        returning      hTmpSemRead
    end-call.

    call winapi "GetCurrentThreadID"
        returning dwParentThreadId
    end-call.

    set ChildThreadProc to entry "ChildThreadProc"
    call winapi "CreateRemoteThread" using
          by value     hRemoteProcess
          by value     0 size 4
          by value     0 size 4
          by value     ChildThreadProc
          by value     0 size 4
          by value     0 size 4
          by reference dwChildThreadId
        returning        hChildThread
    end-call.

   entry "ChildThreadProc" winapi.
   ...................
   ...................

   exit program returning 0.

   call winapi "ReleaseSemaphore" using
          by value hSemRead
          by value 1 size 4
          by value 0 size 4
       returning   return-code
   end-call.

   call winapi "CloseHandle" using
          by value hTmpSemRead
       returning   return-code
   end-call.

*>-------   end   ----------*>
```

```
my
> catch-22 dilemma. Below is the code that I need to work with.

I'd still go with experience (Michael Mattias) vs the manual (MSDN).
Why go out of process when in-process is so much simpler? For example
in Fortran, CALL SYSTEM(command_string) waits until the command
specified finishes before resuming execution. Which makes it, in
effect, a rather large subroutine call!
```

```
Kellie Fitton wrote:
> Hello everyone,
> 
> I have catch-22 dilemma and need your kind help, again.

You sure pick 'em :-). As you are comfortable with APIs, no doubt you 
will follow Michael's suggestions.

Could be done with OO, using just bare bones class syntax, passing 
object handles between the two programs(classes), so that on given 
'events' (i.e. your own defined exit/swap point), each program object 
could invoke the other, back and forth, back and forth......., until 
your final 'EXIT' - triggered by either program.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Can you wakeUp cobol please?...

- **From:** Adrien Plisson <aplisson-news@stochastique.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T10:11:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<421af728$0$23955$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1109021784.833317.81710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Kellie Fitton wrote:

> Hello everyone,
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "WaitForSingleObject", until it returns control back to the parent.
[snip]

don't use WaitForSingleObject. instead try MsgWaitForMultipleObject: 
it is the same as WaitForMultipleObject but also returns if their is 
an event in the message queue. this way, your child program can send a 
message to the parent and should be able to wake it up.

(the actual implementation is left as an exercise for the reader)
```

```
> .. YOU are being emotional rather than objective.

Thank you for demonstrating how highly emotional your postings can be.

> .. YOUr finding about A means nothing in the case of B,
> because A and B are slightly different.

Well, actually your code was just a simple tree and was not an actual
collection class, or even a class, or even OO. You even admitted as
such:

>>> However, your code was not even close to be a collection
>>> class, or even anything to do with classes at all.

RW>> It could easily be turned into a collection class.

Of course you haven't shown us how 'easy' it is. _Everything_ is easy
until you have to actually do it.

> .. The burden is on YOU to write a high-speed collection class.

Well, actually, I doubt that anyone cares whether you do or not, you
just seem to be interested in criticising MF, and, well, the rest of
the world it seems.

Your final comment on the issue seemed to be:

RW>> It's not worth the effort.

It does however, seem to be 'worth the effort' of tracking down the
author in an attempt to parade your self-assigned 'superiority'.  Of
course you haven't actually done a better class yet, but as long as you
haven't you can give it all the superiority you can imagine.

> and its take on a company's  responsibility for its own products?

I am certain that MF takes its responsibilities very seriously. If
there was an actual fault with the collection class, such as it not
working correctly, I am sure that they would rewrite it.

However, your complaint is entirely that it is not as fast as some hand
coded list that isn't generalised, isn't even a class, and has much
less functionality.

No one has forced you to use the MF class, if you object to it then
don't use it.  It is called freedom of choice.

> What does this say about CLC objectivity ..

It seems to me that you have gone out on a complete rant with zero
basis.  There was a message posted a couple of days ago:

RW>> I already demonstrated that performance is piss poor.

JJG> No you haven't. All you showed was you might have
JJG> come up with a piss poor solution.

RW> I take offense when they insult me without
RW> substantiating the disagreement with facts.
RW> For instance, you just did it above when you called my
RW> benchmark of the MF class libraries a piss
RW> poor solution.

It seemed to me that _YOU_ had called the MF collection class 'piss
poor'.  When James said that if you used this class then it would be,
by _YOUR_ own terms, a  'piss poor' solution, you took it as a personal
insult.

Objectivity? you haven't got a clue.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Collection Class speed

```
Robert,

I can't find your original posts as I haven't looked at CLC for a while and
my newsreader is showing summaries on some threads.

I did a search but it revealed nothing under this topic.

You seem to have aroused some very strong emotions, so I'd like to see what
all the fuss is about.

In the meantime, here are some comments on what you've posted here...


"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com...
> Summary of objections to my posting slow speed of MF Collection
> Classes:
>
> .. YOU are in the wrong forum. Stop posting here and complain
> privately to MF Support.

That sounds a bit unfair. They are not mutually exclusive activities; you
could post AND complain.
>
> .. YOU used the wrong tool for the job. Collection classes are
> obviously unsuited to managing <cough> collections of objects.

I'm inclined to think there is more to this than your spin on it... Did you,
in fact, use the MF collection class? If you didn't, then this is fair
comment.

>
> .. YOU are being emotional rather than objective.
>
Well, you're certainly not the only one... <G> I was surprised at Jimmy's
outburst, especially as flaming is not something that is encouraged here. I
guess you touched a few nerves...

> .. The burden is on YOU to write a high-speed collection class.
>

Did anyone actually suggest that? It would be on you if you denigrated the
existing class and stated it could be done better. (It's the old "put up or
shut up" routine... If you don't, then don't expect any credibility...

> .. YOUr finding about A means nothing in the case of B, because A and
> B are slightly different.
>

What part of that seems invalid to you? If I find a lobster is a crayfish, I
cannot criticise lobsters on the basis of my crayfish sample, just because I
THOUGHT it was a lobster...


> Common theme: discredit the messenger (YOU) bearing bad news.
>

Well, sometimes the manner of message presentation can evoke a response that
isn't particularly rational or fair. History shows messengers have been
killed, not so much because of the message they brought (although it was
invariably unplatable to the recipient) but because of the manner in which
they presented it... (Cyrus of Persia executed a messenger on the spot
because he didn't like the man's attire...)

> If results had been favorable -- if Collection Classes had been faster
> or even the same -- none of these objections would have been voiced. I
> would have posted to the right forum, used the right tool, been
> objective, etc.
>

As the Doc pointed out, this is simply conjecture.

(I love the Doc's eloquent imagery though... <G>)

> What does this say about CLC objectivity .. and its take on a
> company's  responsibility for its own products?
>

Hmmm... you have been coming here for a while now. Have you ever found this
forum to be objective? Why would it be? It is a COBOL support forum so it
supports COBOL. However, I have found that the forum will accept and debate
criticism of things COBOL if the arguments are presented fairly and in the
right tone of voice. <G> We have had heated debates here across the years
and I have been involved in some of them. It has rarely descended to
personal abuse.

The bottom line here, Robert, is that if you post to a forum there is no
guarantee of a "fair" hearing. You have no right to expect it, but it is
nice if you get it. You have more chance of getting it if your posts are
moderate and balanced. And, above all, they need to be fair.

As for responsibility for products, you are unlikely to get co-operation
from any company if you publicly criticise their products without talking to
them first. If you really have "discovered" a flaw in MF Collection objects,
your first take should be to talk to the company. That is fair and
reasonable and no less than you would hope if someone found a flaw in
software you had written.

If, having done that, you are met with denial or prevarication or
indifference, then you have a right to "go public". Personally, I would
advise them that I intended to do so and why I was doing it (why go behind
their backs?) and give them a last chance to respond privately.

At the end of the day, if you are really unhappy about the response in CLC,
you have the right to vote with your feet...

Regards,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Collection Class speed

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-02-22T20:59:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109134767.285049.159170@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com>`

```
> If you want to hurt people, don't flame THEM, flame their beloved and
> mediocre IDEAS. Richard understands that.

Well, thank you.

But I am not sure if you are being reflexive and your beloved and
mediocre ideas are being flamed, or whether you are insulting anonymous
other people by saying their ideas are mediocre.

> Richard has suggested that many times. I posted MUCH better
> algorithms. They weren't written as OO classes, but could easily have
> been adapted.

You may well have written a mechanism that works much faster for a very
limited subset of what a collection class should do.  Just as when you
compared your linked list to an array you only measured what the list
could do and not what could be done in an array that your list would
fail at.  For example in an array the 'previous' could be stepped to as
could any arbitrary item.  The list you implemented could only be
accessed serially from the start.  Yes you _could_ add previous
pointers and an array of pointers to the nodes and arbitrary stored
pointers and all manner of this, but this makes it much more complex,
more error prone and slower.  You could add an index in the form of a
tree that is used to access the list with more complexity and time
taken.

> most emotive in my memory was about periods at the end of
> sentences. Mainframers defended them with bulldog tenacity,

Mainframers, and me, defended their right to use them without your
malicious insults.

> while ignoring everything else in my worthy Best Practices style
guide.

Perhaps they didn't think it as worthy as you did.
```

```
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Several of my solutions did use trees. They showed how to balance a
tree by node rotation and they compared the speed of balanced vs.
unbalanced trees. 

Adding backward pointers to a list is easy, especially when done in a
second pass. It's not "much more complex". It's not usually done
because the utility of stepping backward sn't required. 

>> most emotive in my memory was about periods at the end of
>> sentences. Mainframers defended them with bulldog tenacity,

way or be fired.

They didn't read it. They got brain freeze when they got to periods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T10:17:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<559r11t5tiklns73pa92uh02uumhos05ic@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <1109134767.285049.159170@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <ll7q115ect4oc8pqhq20ua5p6t30qqar6b@4ax.com> <1109218412.211741.52150@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 23 Feb 2005 20:13:32 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> It's actually a superset.  I provided a container for
>> the keys. An OO collection class only holds
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>keys ?

When it contains objects.

-- quotation --
The Object COBOL collection classes assume that their elements are
objects. A collection sends messages to its elements to get
information such as the element's size and content. Even when you
store intrinsic data (as opposed to objects), the collection uses the
methods implemented in the intrinsic classes and uses the INVOKE ...AS
verb to send messages to the data.

When you create a dictionary you have to give it a template so that it
knows how the key and data portions are to be stored. The template is
either the Association class, or a clone of the Association class.

The Association class is another clonable class, like the classes for
intrinsic data classes, used for creating templates for data storage.
An Association template actually consists of two templates; one for
the key and one for the data. To create any type of dictionary you
need to create an Association template.

To create an Association template

   1. If the key is to be intrinsic data, clone an intrinsic class to
create a template for the type and length of intrinsic data of your
key.

   2. If the data is to be intrinsic data, clone an intrinsic class to
create a template for the type and length of intrinsic data of your
data.

   3. Clone the Association class. Send it the "newClass" message,
with templates for the key and data part of your association as
parameters. If either of these is an object rather than intrinsic
data, use an object reference set to null as the template.

      If both the key and data entries in the dictionary are to be
objects you can use the Association class itself as a template; you
don't need to create a clone.

Having created a template for your dictionary object, there are two
methods you can use to create a dictionary itself; "ofValues" or
"ofAssociations". A dictionary "ofValues" stores each element as a key
data pair. A dictionary "ofAssociations" stores each element as an
instance of the Association template you used to create the
dictionary.
-- end quotation --

In its simplest form, each dictionary entry points to an association,
and the default Association points to one or two values stored outside
the dictionary.
```

```
defended', in the response to the reply you assert 'Mainframers don't 
defend anything'.  Be that as it may, according to the criterion specified 
in your latest assertion there are no 'mainframers' here that I've seen.

but when you do so you might attempt to recall that the standards used to 
judge poets are not necessarily the standards used to judge programmers.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-02-24T14:39:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cvlahd$ip7$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <1109217555.924142.139630@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <cvkahv$nvt$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109269311.483918.260470@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1109269311.483918.260470@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> what are you calling the 'Roman Inquisition'?
>
>Well what did they call the persecution of the Cathari in the 13th
>century ?

Answering a question with a question, Mr Plinston, is no answer at all... 
but in response to your query I *cannot* resist...

... perhaps they called it 'business as usual'.

While http://www.catholiceducation.org/articles/history/world/wh0029.html 
states:

--begin quoted text:

To remedy the disorganized response to heresy, Pope Gregory IX (1227-41) 
took on the task of bringing the investigation of heresy under the 
discipline of the Holy See. What we term the �Inquisition� is simply the 
ecclesiastical tribunal with specially appointed judges (inquisitors) 
answerable to both the local bishop and the pope, whose task it was to 
investigate charges of heresy in a systematic and fair way. The origin of 
this form of judicial inquiry, the inquisitio, was not Church law, but 
Roman law as incorporated into the procedures of civil and canon law 
alike. Pope Gregory wisely relied on the new mendicant orders, the 
Franciscans and the Dominicans, to handle most of the inquisitorial work.

--end quoted text

... it goes on later to state:

--begin quoted text:

With the advent of the Reformation in the 16th century, another phase of 
the Inquisition began. Alarmed at the spread of Protestantism, Pope Paul 
III (1534-49) established the Roman Inquisition in 1542. Perhaps its most 
famous act was the conviction of Galileo for violating its injunction of 
1616 that he neither teach nor defend the thesis that the sun is the 
immovable center of the universe.

--end quoted text

... so at least according to the Catholic Educator's Resource Center the 
Roman Inquisition was established in 1542.

DD
```
